name: Barbell Bench Press
description: Setup: Lie flat on bench with eyes under the bar, feet firmly planted on floor. Grip bar slightly wider than shoulder-width with wrists straight. Technique: Unrack and position bar over chest. Lower with controlled tempo to mid-chest, keeping elbows at 45° angle. Touch chest lightly, then press explosively while maintaining scapular retraction. Hints: Engage lats by 'bending the bar' to create tension. Keep shoulder blades pinched throughout. Drive through legs for stability. Warnings: Avoid bouncing bar off chest. Don't flare elbows to 90° (shoulder injury risk). Never lift feet during heavy sets.

name: Barbell Squats
description: Setup: Bar on upper traps (high bar) or rear delts (low bar). Feet shoulder-width or slightly wider, toes 15-30° outward. Technique: Brace core hard, initiate by pushing hips back while simultaneously bending knees. Descend until thighs parallel or deeper if mobility allows. Knees track over toes. Drive through full foot to ascend, leading with chest. Hints: Focus on sitting 'between' your legs, not just back. Keep chest up and neutral spine. Maintain upright torso for high bar, more forward lean for low bar. Warnings: Never let knees cave inward (valgus collapse). Don't round lower back—stop depth if form breaks. Ensure full depth control before adding weight.

name: Deadlifts
description: Setup: Bar over mid-foot, shins nearly touching. Hip-width stance, hands just outside legs. Technique: Hinge at hips, grip bar, flatten back, and create tension before lifting. Drive through floor with legs while maintaining neutral spine. Bar travels in straight vertical line, brushing legs. Fully extend hips and knees at top. Reverse motion under control. Hints: Think 'leg press the floor away' rather than pulling. Keep bar against body throughout. Lats engaged ('protect your armpits'). Neck neutral. Warnings: Never round lower back under load. Don't jerk or yank the bar—pull slack out first. Avoid hyperextending at top. Drop hips too low and it becomes a squat (inefficient).

name: Barbell Rows
description: Setup: Hip hinge position with torso 30-45° to floor. Grip bar shoulder-width (or wider for variety). Knees slightly bent, back neutral. Technique: Pull bar to lower chest/upper abdomen, leading with elbows. Squeeze shoulder blades together at top. Lower with control, fully extending arms. Keep torso angle constant. Hints: Don't use momentum or body English. Initiate pull with lats, not arms. Think 'elbows back and up.' Retract scapula at peak contraction. Warnings: Avoid excessive torso swing (cheating reduces effectiveness). Don't round lower back—maintain neutral spine. Keep neck neutral, not hyperextended.

name: Overhead Press
description: Setup: Bar racked at upper chest, hands just outside shoulders. Elbows slightly forward of bar. Feet hip-width, core braced. Technique: Press bar straight up, moving head back slightly to clear path. Fully extend arms overhead with bar over mid-foot. Bring head through at top. Lower with control to starting position. Hints: Keep wrists stacked over elbows. Squeeze glutes to prevent back hyperextension. Press in vertical line, not forward arc. Full lockout at top. Warnings: Never hyperextend lower back to compensate for limited mobility or excessive weight. Don't press the bar forward—it should travel vertically. Avoid leaning back excessively.

name: Pull-ups
description: Setup: Hang from bar with hands shoulder-width or slightly wider, palms facing away. Full dead hang with arms extended. Technique: Engage lats and pull elbows down and back, bringing chin over bar. Control descent to full extension. Avoid swinging or kipping. Hints: Depress shoulders ('pull shoulders into back pockets') before initiating pull. Lead with chest, not chin. Cross feet for stability. Think 'pull bar to chest' not 'chin to bar.' Warnings: Don't kip or use momentum (unless training CrossFit-style). Avoid shrugging shoulders up. Don't crane neck to get chin over—use full back engagement.

name: Dips
description: Setup: Support body on parallel bars, arms fully extended, shoulders depressed. Lean forward 10-20° for chest emphasis, more upright for triceps. Technique: Lower body by bending elbows until upper arms parallel to ground (or slightly below). Press back up to full extension. Control tempo on descent. Hints: Keep elbows at 45° angle for chest activation. Don't flare elbows wide. Maintain forward lean throughout. Engage core to prevent swinging. Warnings: Don't descend too deep if shoulders lack mobility (risk of anterior shoulder strain). Avoid shrugging shoulders at top. Stop if you feel sharp shoulder pain.

name: Leg Press
description: Setup: Sit with back and head against pad. Feet shoulder-width on platform, mid-foot centered. Technique: Release safety, lower platform under control until knees at 90° (or deeper if mobility allows without back rounding). Press through full foot, extending knees and hips. Don't lock out completely. Hints: Keep lower back flat against pad—no rounding or arching. Knees track over toes. Adjust foot position: higher for glutes/hams, lower for quads. Control eccentric phase. Warnings: Never let lower back lift off pad during descent (disc injury risk). Don't lock knees at top. Avoid bouncing at bottom.

name: Leg Curls
description: Setup: Adjust machine so pad rests on lower calves, just above ankles. Knees align with machine pivot. Lie face down, grip handles. Technique: Curl legs by flexing knees, bringing heels toward glutes. Squeeze hamstrings at peak contraction. Lower with control, stopping just before stack touches. Hints: Don't use hip flexion to cheat the weight up. Keep hips pressed to pad. Point toes or flex feet for variation. Pause briefly at top. Warnings: Avoid jerking or using momentum. Don't hyperextend lower back by lifting hips. If experiencing knee pain, check pad placement.

name: Walking Lunges
description: Setup: Stand upright with dumbbells at sides or holding single weight at chest. Feet hip-width. Technique: Step forward with one leg, lowering hips until both knees at 90°. Front knee over ankle, back knee hovering above ground. Drive through front heel to step forward with opposite leg. Hints: Keep torso upright. Front shin vertical. Take controlled, moderate-length steps. Maintain balance by engaging core. Alternate legs each step. Warnings: Don't let front knee collapse inward or shoot forward past toes. Avoid leaning forward excessively. Don't step too far (overextension) or too short (reduced effectiveness).

name: Romanian Deadlifts
description: Setup: Stand holding bar at hip height, shoulder-width grip. Feet hip-width, slight knee bend. Technique: Hinge at hips, pushing them back while maintaining neutral spine. Lower bar down front of thighs until hamstrings feel stretched (typically mid-shin). Drive hips forward to return to start. Bar stays close to body. Hints: This is a hip hinge, not a squat—minimal knee bend. Feel stretch in hamstrings, not lower back. Keep shoulders back, chest up. Neck neutral. Warnings: Never round lower back. Don't squat the weight—hips move back, not down. Avoid overextending at top or dropping bar too low if flexibility is limited.

name: Bicep Curls
description: Setup: Stand with dumbbells at sides, palms forward (supinated grip). Elbows close to torso. Technique: Curl weights up by flexing elbows, keeping upper arms stationary. Squeeze biceps at top. Lower with control to full extension. Avoid swinging. Hints: Keep elbows pinned to sides—only forearms move. Don't lean back or use body momentum. Supinate wrists fully at top for peak contraction. Control eccentric phase (3 seconds down). Warnings: Don't swing weights or use hip thrust. Avoid partial reps—full extension to full contraction. Don't let elbows drift forward.

name: Tricep Extensions
description: Setup: Face cable machine with rope or bar attachment at high position. Grip attachment, elbows at sides bent 90°. Technique: Extend elbows, pressing attachment down until arms fully straight. Keep elbows pinned to sides. Return with control, stopping at 90° or just before stack touches. Hints: Lean slightly forward for stability. Focus on elbow extension, not pressing. Keep shoulders down and back. Squeeze triceps at full extension. Warnings: Don't let elbows flare out to sides. Avoid using shoulder or lat involvement to push weight. Don't lean excessively forward or use body weight.

name: Lat Pulldown
description: Setup: Sit with thighs secured under pad. Grip bar slightly wider than shoulder-width, palms forward. Technique: Pull bar to upper chest by driving elbows down and back. Lean back slightly (10-15°). Squeeze lats at bottom. Return with control to full arm extension. Hints: Initiate with lats, not arms—think 'elbows to back pockets.' Keep chest up. Don't use momentum or excessive lean back. Retract scapula throughout. Warnings: Avoid pulling bar behind neck (shoulder injury risk). Don't lean back more than 15°. Never use jerking motion or drop weight uncontrolled.

name: Cable Rows
description: Setup: Sit at cable station with feet on platform, knees slightly bent. Grip handle, arms extended. Technique: Pull handle to lower chest/upper abdomen, driving elbows back. Keep torso upright (slight lean back acceptable). Squeeze shoulder blades together. Extend arms with control. Hints: Maintain neutral spine. Don't round or hyperextend back. Focus on scapular retraction at peak. Keep shoulders down. Think 'pull elbows, not hands.' Warnings: Avoid excessive torso swing or momentum. Don't round lower back. Keep movement controlled—don't let weight pull you forward.

name: Dumbbell Bench Press
description: Setup: Lie on bench with dumbbells at chest level, elbows at 45°. Feet flat on floor. Technique: Press dumbbells up and slightly together, stopping just before they touch. Lower with control until elbows slightly below torso level. Maintain constant tension. Hints: Allow greater range of motion than barbell version. Keep wrists straight. Press in slight arc, not straight up. Squeeze chest at top. Control descent to get full stretch. Warnings: Don't bang dumbbells together at top. Avoid dropping elbows too low if shoulder mobility is limited. Never bounce weights off chest.

name: Incline Bench Press
description: Setup: Bench at 30-45° incline (30° optimal for upper chest). Grip bar slightly wider than shoulders. Technique: Unrack and lower bar to upper chest (near clavicles). Press explosively while maintaining scapular retraction. Bar path slightly diagonal. Hints: Lower incline angle (30°) targets chest better than steep (45°+ becomes more shoulders). Keep shoulder blades pinched. Feet flat for leg drive. Elbows at 45-60° angle. Warnings: Don't go too steep (reduces chest activation). Avoid flaring elbows to 90°. Never bounce bar off chest.

name: Decline Bench Press
description: Setup: Bench declined 15-30°, legs secured. Grip bar slightly wider than shoulders. Technique: Unrack and lower to lower chest/sternum area. Press up in slight arc toward face. Full extension at top. Hints: Shorter range of motion than flat bench—can typically handle more weight. Targets lower/mid chest. Maintain scapular retraction. Don't decline too steeply (reduces effectiveness). Warnings: Ensure legs are secure before unracking. Don't bounce bar. Avoid excessive decline angle.

name: Chest Flyes
description: Setup: Lie on bench holding dumbbells above chest, arms extended with slight elbow bend. Technique: Lower dumbbells in wide arc, maintaining elbow angle. Descend until chest stretch felt. Bring dumbbells back together using chest, maintaining arm arc. Hints: This is an isolation move—keep elbow angle constant (don't turn into press). Feel stretch at bottom. Squeeze pecs at top. Use lighter weight than presses. Warnings: Don't straighten arms completely (elbow joint stress). Avoid going too deep if shoulder mobility is limited. Never use heavy weight or jerky movements.

name: Pec Deck
description: Setup: Adjust seat so handles at chest height. Sit with back against pad, forearms on pads or gripping handles. Technique: Bring arms together in front of chest using pec contraction. Squeeze hard at peak. Return with control to stretched position. Hints: Focus on chest squeeze, not arm movement. Keep slight elbow bend. Don't use momentum. Pause and contract at peak. Adjust seat for proper arm alignment. Warnings: Don't overstretch at starting position if shoulder mobility is limited. Avoid slamming weight stack. Keep movement controlled.

name: Leg Extensions
description: Setup: Adjust machine so knee pivot aligns with machine pivot. Pad on lower shins. Sit with back against pad. Technique: Extend knees to lift weight, squeezing quads at top. Hold briefly. Lower with control, stopping before stack touches. Hints: Point toes forward or slightly out. Don't lock knees at top—stop just before. Focus on quad contraction. Adjust seat back for comfort. Warnings: Avoid slamming weight at bottom. Never use momentum or jerky movements (knee joint stress). If experiencing knee pain, reduce weight or check form.

name: Calf Raises
description: Setup: Position shoulders under pads, balls of feet on platform edge, heels hanging off. Technique: Lower heels below platform for full stretch. Press up onto toes as high as possible, squeezing calves at top. Hold peak contraction 1-2 seconds. Lower with control. Hints: Full range of motion crucial for calf development. Pause at top and bottom. Keep knees locked (standing) or bent (seated for soleus focus). Toes forward, in, or out for variation. Warnings: Don't bounce at bottom (Achilles strain risk). Avoid partial reps. Use controlled tempo throughout.

name: Lateral Raises
description: Setup: Stand with dumbbells at sides, slight elbow bend, palms facing body. Technique: Raise dumbbells out to sides in arc motion until arms parallel to floor. Lead with elbows, not hands. Lower with control. Keep torso still. Hints: Think 'pouring water from pitcher' at top—slight forward tilt of dumbbells. Keep elbows slightly higher than wrists. Don't swing or use momentum. Use lighter weight for strict form. Warnings: Don't raise above shoulder height (reduces delt tension, increases trap involvement). Avoid shrugging shoulders. Never swing weights or lean side to side.

name: Front Raises
description: Setup: Stand holding dumbbells in front of thighs, palms facing body or down. Slight elbow bend. Technique: Raise dumbbells forward and up to shoulder height, maintaining arm angle. Lower with control. Keep core braced. Hints: Can alternate arms or lift together. Keep shoulders down. Don't swing weights. Stop at shoulder height. Use lighter weight than lateral raises. Warnings: Avoid raising above shoulder level. Don't lean back or use momentum. Never lock elbows.

name: Face Pulls
description: Setup: Rope attachment at face height. Grip ends of rope with palms facing each other. Step back for tension. Technique: Pull rope toward face, separating handles beside head. Elbows high and out. Squeeze rear delts and mid-traps. Return with control. Hints: Focus on pulling elbows back, not hands. External rotation at end of movement. Keep shoulders down. This targets rear delts and upper back. Use moderate weight. Warnings: Don't use excessive weight—compromises form. Avoid pulling with arms only. Keep movement smooth and controlled.

name: Shrugs
description: Setup: Stand holding dumbbells at sides, arms straight, shoulders down. Technique: Elevate shoulders straight up toward ears as high as possible. Squeeze traps at top. Lower with control to full stretch. Keep arms straight throughout. Hints: Think 'shoulders to ears,' not back. Don't roll shoulders (injury risk). Hold peak contraction 1-2 seconds. Can use heavy weight. Warnings: Never roll shoulders forward or backward. Avoid bending elbows. Don't use momentum or jerky movements.

name: Hammer Curls
description: Setup: Stand with dumbbells at sides, palms facing body (neutral grip). Technique: Curl weights up keeping thumbs on top throughout. Elbows stay at sides. Squeeze at top. Lower with control. Hints: This targets brachialis and brachioradialis more than standard curls. Keep wrists straight. Maintain neutral grip throughout. Can alternate arms or curl together. Warnings: Don't swing or use momentum. Keep elbows pinned. Avoid leaning back.

name: Preacher Curls
description: Setup: Sit at preacher bench, armpits resting on top pad. Arms extended over angled pad, gripping bar. Technique: Curl bar up, squeezing biceps at top. Lower with control to full extension without letting elbows lift off pad. Hints: Pad angle isolates biceps by preventing shoulder involvement. Keep triceps in contact with pad. Full range of motion crucial. Can use EZ-bar for wrist comfort. Warnings: Don't lift elbows off pad or lean back. Avoid dropping weight quickly (bicep tear risk). Never hyperextend elbows at bottom.

name: Skull Crushers
description: Setup: Lie on bench holding bar above chest, arms extended. Angle arms back slightly (not straight vertical). Technique: Lower bar toward forehead/behind head by bending elbows. Keep upper arms stationary. Extend back to start. Hints: Upper arms should be angled back ~15° from vertical for constant tension. Elbows stay in—don't flare. Can lower to forehead, nose, or behind head. EZ-bar easier on wrists. Warnings: Keep elbows stable—don't let them drift forward or flare out. Use controlled tempo to protect elbows. Avoid excessive weight.

name: Close Grip Bench Press
description: Setup: Lie on bench, grip bar with hands 6-12 inches apart (inside shoulder width). Technique: Lower bar to lower chest/sternum with elbows tucked close to sides. Press up explosively. Elbows stay at ~30° from body. Hints: This emphasizes triceps while still working chest. Keep elbows tucked—not flared. Don't grip too narrow (wrist strain). Full lockout at top. Warnings: Avoid grip narrower than 6 inches (wrist/elbow stress). Don't let elbows flare wide. Keep wrists straight.

name: Chin-ups
description: Setup: Hang from bar with hands shoulder-width, palms facing you (supinated/underhand grip). Technique: Pull up by driving elbows down, bringing chin over bar. Squeeze lats and biceps at top. Lower with control to full extension. Hints: Supinated grip emphasizes biceps more than pull-ups. Depress shoulders before pulling. Keep core tight. Can cross feet for stability. Warnings: Don't kip or swing. Avoid shrugging shoulders. Don't crane neck to get chin over.

name: T-Bar Rows
description: Setup: Straddle loaded barbell (one end anchored or in corner). Grip handle attachment or bar. Hip hinge position, knees bent. Technique: Pull bar to chest, driving elbows back. Squeeze lats and mid-back at top. Lower with control. Keep torso angle stable. Hints: Wider grip targets lats, closer grip hits mid-back more. Keep chest up. Use neutral or pronated grip. Retract scapula at peak. Warnings: Don't round lower back. Avoid excessive torso movement. Keep core braced throughout.

name: Hyperextensions
description: Setup: Position hips on pad of hyperextension bench, feet secured. Start with torso perpendicular to floor or slightly lower. Technique: Hinge at hips to lower torso toward floor while maintaining neutral spine. Raise back up by contracting glutes and lower back to horizontal position. Hints: Focus on hip hinge movement, not spinal extension. Squeeze glutes at top. Can hold weight at chest for resistance. Keep neck neutral. Warnings: Don't hyperextend spine excessively at top—stop at horizontal. Avoid rounding back. If used for lower back, keep movement controlled.

name: Plank
description: Setup: Forearms on floor, elbows under shoulders. Extend legs, supporting on toes. Body forms straight line. Technique: Hold position while maintaining neutral spine, engaged core, and squeezed glutes. Breathe normally. Hints: Don't let hips sag or pike up. Keep head neutral, looking at floor. Squeeze glutes and quads to maintain position. Start with 30-60 second holds. Warnings: Don't hold breath. Avoid letting lower back sag (puts stress on spine). Stop if form breaks.

name: Cable Crunches
description: Setup: Kneel facing cable machine with rope attachment at high position. Hold rope ends beside head. Technique: Crunch down by flexing spine, bringing elbows toward thighs. Contract abs hard at bottom. Return with control, maintaining tension. Hints: Movement comes from spinal flexion, not hip flexion. Keep hips stationary. Focus on abs contraction, not pulling with arms. Exhale on crunch. Warnings: Don't pull with arms or use momentum. Avoid excessive weight that causes form breakdown. Keep movement controlled.

name: Russian Twists
description: Setup: Sit with knees bent, feet off floor, torso at 45° angle. Hold weight at chest or clasp hands together. Technique: Rotate torso side to side, touching weight/hands to floor beside hips. Keep core engaged throughout. Move with control. Hints: Lean back to increase difficulty. Keep chest up and spine neutral. Focus on rotation from obliques. Can keep feet on floor for easier variation. Warnings: Don't round spine excessively. Avoid using momentum to swing. Keep movement controlled to engage obliques properly.

name: Pendulum Squat
description: Setup: Position yourself on the swing squat machine with back against pad, feet on platform shoulder-width apart. Technique: Lower body by bending knees while keeping back pressed to pad. Descend until thighs parallel or deeper. Drive through heels to return to start. Hints: Machine provides guided movement pattern. Keep core engaged. Full range of motion for maximum effectiveness. Warnings: Don't let knees cave inward. Avoid locking out completely at top. Keep lower back against pad.

name: Abdominal Machine
description: Setup: Sit on machine with chest pad adjusted to mid-chest level. Grip handles, feet secured. Technique: Crunch forward by flexing spine, bringing chest toward thighs. Contract abs fully. Return with control without letting weight stack touch. Hints: Focus on ab contraction, not pulling with arms. Exhale on crunch. Keep movement smooth and controlled. Adjust weight to allow 12-15 quality reps. Warnings: Don't use momentum or jerky movements. Avoid excessive weight that forces poor form. Keep neck neutral.

name: Bench Press Machine
description: Setup: Adjust seat so handles align with mid-chest. Sit with back flat against pad, feet on floor. Technique: Press handles forward until arms extended. Return with control, stopping before weight stack touches. Keep shoulder blades retracted. Hints: Machine provides stable path for pressing. Good for training to failure safely. Can focus purely on chest contraction. Keep elbows at 45° angle. Warnings: Don't bounce at bottom position. Avoid flaring elbows to 90°. Keep wrists straight.

name: Rope Pushdown
description: Setup: Attach rope to high cable pulley. Grip rope ends with thumbs up, elbows at sides. Technique: Push rope down by extending elbows. At bottom, split rope ends apart for extra tricep contraction. Return with control to 90° elbow bend. Hints: Keep elbows pinned to sides. Split rope at bottom maximizes tricep engagement. Lean slightly forward for stability. Full extension crucial. Warnings: Don't let elbows drift forward or flare out. Avoid using body weight or momentum. Keep shoulders down.

name: Triceps Press Machine
description: Setup: Adjust seat so arms align with pivot point. Sit with upper arms on pad, hands gripping handles. Technique: Extend elbows to press handles down. Squeeze triceps at full extension. Return with control without letting weight stack touch. Hints: Machine isolates triceps effectively. Keep upper arms pressed to pads. Focus on elbow extension only. Warnings: Don't let elbows leave pads. Avoid jerky movements. Use controlled tempo.

name: Rope Skull Crushers
description: Setup: Use rope on low cable pulley. Lie on bench with head near cable, arms extended overhead holding rope. Technique: Lower rope toward forehead/behind head by bending elbows. Keep upper arms stationary. Extend back to start. Hints: Constant cable tension throughout movement. Keep elbows tucked, not flared. Can lower to forehead or behind head. Split rope at bottom for variation. Warnings: Keep upper arms stable—don't let them drift. Use controlled tempo to protect elbows. Avoid excessive weight.

name: Cable Lateral Raise
description: Setup: Stand sideways to cable machine, handle in far hand at hip level. Technique: Raise arm out to side until parallel to floor. Lead with elbow. Lower with control. Constant tension from cable. Hints: Slight bend in elbow. Think 'pouring water' at top. Keep shoulders down. Cable provides constant resistance unlike dumbbells. Warnings: Don't raise above shoulder height. Avoid shrugging. No swinging or momentum.

name: Rope Face Pull
description: Setup: Rope attachment at face height. Grip rope ends, step back for tension. Technique: Pull rope toward face, separating handles beside head. Elbows high and out. External rotation at end. Squeeze rear delts and mid-traps. Hints: Focus on pulling elbows back, not hands. Keep shoulders down. Great for rear delt and posture. Use moderate weight for proper form. Warnings: Don't use excessive weight that compromises form. Avoid pulling with arms only. Keep movement smooth.

name: Bent Over Rows
description: Setup: Hip hinge with torso at 45° angle. Grip barbell shoulder-width, knees slightly bent, back neutral. Technique: Pull bar to lower chest, driving elbows back. Squeeze shoulder blades at top. Lower with control to full arm extension. Hints: Maintain consistent torso angle. Lead pull with elbows. Keep bar close to body. Retract scapula at peak. Warnings: Never round lower back. Don't use excessive momentum. Keep neck neutral. Avoid standing too upright.

name: Close Grip Pulldown
description: Setup: Use close grip handle (palms facing). Sit with thighs secured, arms extended overhead. Technique: Pull handle to upper chest by driving elbows down. Squeeze lats at bottom. Return with control to full extension. Hints: Close grip emphasizes lower lats. Keep chest up. Focus on lat engagement, not arm pull. Slight lean back acceptable. Warnings: Don't use excessive lean. Avoid jerky movements. Keep shoulders down and back.

name: Seated Incline Dumbbell Curl
description: Setup: Set bench to 45-60° incline. Sit with back against pad, dumbbells hanging at sides with arms fully extended. Technique: Curl weights up, keeping upper arms perpendicular to floor. Squeeze biceps at top. Lower with control to full extension. Hints: Incline provides increased stretch on biceps. Keep elbows stationary—only forearms move. Full range of motion crucial. Can alternate arms or curl together. Warnings: Don't let elbows drift forward. Avoid swinging or using momentum. Keep back against pad.

name: Rope Hammer Curl
description: Setup: Rope on low cable pulley. Stand facing cable, grip rope ends with neutral grip (palms facing). Technique: Curl rope up keeping elbows at sides, maintaining neutral grip throughout. Squeeze at top. Lower with control. Hints: Constant cable tension. Targets brachialis and brachioradialis. Keep elbows pinned. Can separate rope ends at top. Warnings: Don't swing or use momentum. Avoid letting elbows drift forward. Keep wrists straight.

name: Cable Wrist Curl
description: Setup: Attach straight bar to low cable. Kneel or sit facing cable, forearms on bench with hands hanging off edge, palms up. Technique: Curl wrists up by flexing forearms. Squeeze at top. Lower with control. Keep forearms stationary. Hints: Isolates forearm flexors. Use light weight for high reps (15-20). Full range of motion. Can also do reverse grip for extensors. Warnings: Don't use heavy weight or jerky movements. Avoid letting forearms lift off support. Keep movement controlled.

name: Rear Delt Fly
description: Setup: Bend forward at hips to torso parallel with floor. Dumbbells hanging down, slight elbow bend. Technique: Raise dumbbells out to sides in arc motion. Lead with elbows. Squeeze rear delts at top. Lower with control. Hints: Keep torso stable and parallel. Focus on rear delt contraction. Don't use momentum. Can also do seated bent over. Warnings: Don't round lower back. Avoid swinging weights. Keep slight elbow bend constant.

name: Cable One Arm Lateral Raise
description: Setup: Stand sideways to cable, handle in far hand at hip level. Can hold support with near hand. Technique: Raise arm out to side to shoulder height. Lead with elbow. Lower with control. Complete set then switch sides. Hints: Cable provides constant tension. Focus on one side allows better mind-muscle connection. Keep shoulder down. Slight lean away acceptable. Warnings: Don't raise above shoulder height. Avoid momentum or swinging. Keep movement controlled.

name: Barbell Pullover
description: Setup: Lie perpendicular on bench with only upper back supported. Hold barbell above chest with arms slightly bent. Technique: Lower barbell back and over head in arc until arms parallel with torso. Feel stretch in lats. Pull back to start using lats. Hints: Keep arms slightly bent throughout. Focus on lat engagement, not arm movement. Can also target chest depending on technique. Control the stretch. Warnings: Don't overstretch if flexibility limited. Keep hips down. Avoid excessive weight until form mastered.

name: Pec Fly Machine
description: Setup: Adjust seat so handles at chest height. Sit with back against pad, grip handles with slight elbow bend. Technique: Bring handles together in front of chest using pec contraction. Squeeze hard at peak. Return with control to stretch position. Hints: Isolation exercise for chest. Focus on squeeze, not pressing. Keep slight elbow bend constant. Pause at peak contraction. Warnings: Don't overstretch at start if shoulder mobility limited. Avoid bouncing or using momentum. Keep movement controlled.

name: Low to High Cable Fly
description: Setup: Cables at low position. Stand in center holding handles, step forward into split stance. Technique: Raise handles up and together in arc motion to face level. Squeeze pecs at top. Lower with control. Targets upper chest. Hints: Maintain slight elbow bend. Focus on upper chest contraction. Keep torso stable. Can adjust height for different angles. Warnings: Don't use momentum or swing. Avoid straightening arms completely. Keep core engaged for stability.

name: Seated Cable Fly
description: Setup: Adjust seat between cable stations at chest height. Sit with back against pad, holding handles with arms extended to sides. Technique: Bring handles together in front of chest using pec contraction. Squeeze at center. Return with control to stretched position. Hints: Seated position provides stability. Constant cable tension throughout. Slight elbow bend. Focus on chest squeeze. Warnings: Don't overstretch at start. Avoid bouncing. Keep wrists straight and stable.

name: Barbell Overhand Bent Over Rows
description: Setup: Hip hinge position, torso 45° angle. Overhand grip (palms down) on barbell, hands shoulder-width. Technique: Pull bar to lower chest, driving elbows back and up. Squeeze lats and mid-back at top. Lower with control. Keep torso angle constant. Hints: Overhand grip emphasizes upper back more than underhand. Keep bar close to body. Initiate with lats. Retract scapula fully. Warnings: Maintain neutral spine throughout. Don't use excessive body English. Keep neck neutral. Avoid rounding back.

name: Pullover with Machine
description: Setup: Adjust seat so shoulders align with pivot point. Sit with back against pad, place elbows on pads, grip handles. Technique: Pull arms down in arc motion, bringing elbows toward hips. Squeeze lats at bottom. Return with control to start position. Hints: Keep arms slightly bent throughout. Focus on lat engagement. Don't use momentum. Full range of motion for maximum lat stretch and contraction. Warnings: Don't overstretch at top position. Avoid pulling with arms instead of lats. Keep core engaged and torso stable.

name: Seated Leg Curl
description: Setup: Adjust machine so pad rests on lower calves, just above ankles. Sit with back against pad, thighs secured under pad. Technique: Curl legs by flexing knees, bringing heels toward glutes. Squeeze hamstrings at peak contraction. Lower with control. Hints: Don't use hip flexion to cheat. Keep torso stable against pad. Point toes or flex feet for variation. Pause briefly at top. Warnings: Avoid jerking or using momentum. Don't round back. If experiencing knee pain, check pad placement.

name: Seated Calf
description: Setup: Sit at seated calf machine with knees under pads, balls of feet on platform. Technique: Lower heels below platform for full stretch. Press up onto toes as high as possible, squeezing calves at top. Hold peak contraction 1-2 seconds. Lower with control. Hints: Seated position targets soleus muscle. Full range of motion crucial. Pause at top and bottom. Keep movement controlled. Warnings: Don't bounce at bottom (Achilles strain risk). Avoid partial reps. Use controlled tempo throughout.

name: Incline Press
description: Setup: Bench at 30-45° incline. Grip bar slightly wider than shoulders. Technique: Unrack and lower bar to upper chest. Press explosively while maintaining scapular retraction. Bar path slightly diagonal. Hints: Lower incline angle (30°) targets chest better than steep angles. Keep shoulder blades pinched. Elbows at 45-60° angle. Warnings: Don't go too steep (reduces chest activation). Avoid flaring elbows to 90°. Never bounce bar off chest.

name: Barbell Pulldown
description: Setup: Use barbell attached to cable or machine at high position. Grip bar with hands shoulder-width or slightly wider. Technique: Pull bar down to upper chest by driving elbows down and back. Squeeze lats at bottom. Return with control to full arm extension. Keep chest up and shoulders back. Hints: Similar to lat pulldown but using barbell for different grip variation. Focus on lat engagement. Maintain upright torso or slight lean back. Keep shoulders down throughout. Warnings: Don't use excessive momentum. Avoid leaning back more than 15°. Keep movement controlled and smooth.

name: Calf Press on Leg Press
description: Setup: Sit in leg press machine with back against pad. Place balls of feet on lower edge of platform, heels hanging off. Legs extended but not locked. Technique: Lower heels below platform edge for full calf stretch. Press through balls of feet, extending ankles as high as possible. Squeeze calves at top. Hold peak contraction 1-2 seconds. Lower with control. Hints: Keep legs nearly straight throughout—this is an ankle movement, not a leg press. Use lighter weight than regular leg press. Full range of motion crucial. Vary toe angles (in, out, straight) to target different calf areas. Warnings: Don't lock knees completely. Avoid bouncing at bottom (Achilles strain risk). Keep safety catches engaged. Never use excessive weight that compromises control.

name: Cable Crunch
description: Setup: Attach rope handle to high cable pulley. Kneel facing cable machine, grip rope handles beside head or behind neck. Technique: Keeping hips stationary, crunch down by flexing spine, bringing elbows toward thighs. Focus on contracting abs to pull weight down, not arms. Squeeze abs at bottom. Return with control to starting position. Hints: Think of curling ribcage toward pelvis. Keep arms in fixed position—movement comes from spine flexion only. Don't sit back on heels during crunch. Exhale on contraction, inhale on return. Warnings: Don't use hip flexion to pull weight down. Avoid jerky movements. Keep neck neutral—don't tuck chin excessively. If lower back hurts, check form or reduce weight.

name: Single Arm Rear Delt Fly
description: Setup: Set cable pulley to shoulder height. Stand sideways to machine, grasp handle with far hand across body. Slight bend in working elbow. Free hand can hold machine for stability. Technique: Pull handle in arc motion away from body, leading with elbow. Focus on rear delt contraction—squeeze at end position. Return with control, maintaining tension. Keep torso stable throughout. Hints: Think of pulling elbow back and out, not just moving the hand. Unilateral training helps identify and correct strength imbalances. Keep shoulder blade stable—don't over-retract. Use lighter weight for strict form. Warnings: Don't rotate torso to assist the movement. Avoid using momentum or swinging. Keep wrist neutral. Don't shrug shoulder during the lift.

name: Single-Arm Cable Lat Pulldown
description: Setup: Attach single handle to high cable pulley. Sit on bench facing away from the machine or straddle the bench facing forward, using it to stabilize your torso. Grip handle with one hand, arm fully extended overhead. Technique: Pull handle down by driving elbow straight down alongside your body. Focus on lat contraction—squeeze at bottom when elbow is at your side. Return with control to full arm extension. Complete all reps on one side before switching. Hints: Keep torso stable by bracing against the bench—this isolates the lat and prevents cheating. Think 'elbow to hip pocket' rather than pulling with the hand. Maintain slight forward lean for optimal lat engagement. Unilateral training helps identify and correct strength imbalances between sides. Warnings: Don't rotate torso to assist the movement. Avoid using momentum or jerky motions. Keep shoulder down—don't shrug during the pull. Control the eccentric phase to maximize muscle tension.

name: Adduction Machine
description: Setup: Sit on machine with back against pad, legs spread apart on the leg pads. Adjust starting position width if available. Grip handles for stability. Technique: Squeeze legs together by contracting inner thigh muscles (adductors). Pause and squeeze at the center position. Return with control to starting position, stopping before weight stack touches. Hints: Focus on squeezing through the inner thighs, not just moving the legs. Keep back flat against pad throughout. Control both the concentric and eccentric phases. Adjust weight to allow 12-15 quality reps. Warnings: Don't use momentum or jerky movements. Avoid going too wide at start if flexibility is limited. Keep movement controlled to prevent groin strain. Stop if you feel sharp pain in inner thigh or groin area.

name: Abduction Machine
description: Setup: Sit on machine with back against pad, legs together on the leg pads. Adjust starting position if available. Grip handles for stability. Technique: Push legs apart by contracting outer thigh muscles (abductors). Pause and squeeze at the widest position. Return with control to starting position, stopping before weight stack touches. Hints: Focus on pushing through the outer thighs, not just moving the legs. Keep back flat against pad throughout. Control both the concentric and eccentric phases. Adjust weight to allow 12-15 quality reps. Warnings: Don't use momentum or jerky movements. Avoid going too wide if flexibility is limited. Keep movement controlled to prevent strain. Stop if you feel sharp pain in hip or outer thigh area.

name: Hammer Strength High Row
description: Setup: Sit facing the chest pad with upper chest resting against it. Grip the overhead handles with palms facing down—arms angled upward at roughly 45°. Feet flat on floor. Technique: Pull handles downward and back in an arc toward your upper ribcage, leading with elbows. Squeeze upper back and rear delts at peak contraction. Return with control. Hints: This specifically targets upper lats, mid-traps, and rear delts—a different angle than standard rows or pulldowns. Keep chest pressed into pad to eliminate momentum. Think 'pull elbows into back pockets at an angle.' The high-pull path is unique to this machine and hard to replicate with free weights. Warnings: Don't lean back away from the pad. Avoid shrugging shoulders—keep them depressed. Don't use momentum or jerk the weight.

name: Hammer Strength V-Squat
description: Setup: Stand on the angled platform with shoulders under the pads. Feet shoulder-width, positioned forward on the platform. Release safety handles. Technique: Descend by bending knees and hips, keeping torso guided along the fixed V-shaped track. Lower until thighs are at or below parallel. Drive through heels to stand back up. The angled track keeps your torso more upright than a barbell squat. Hints: The fixed path reduces spinal loading compared to free-weight squats while still hitting quads and glutes hard. Adjust foot placement—higher for more glute/hamstring, lower for quad emphasis. The V-angle uniquely combines squat and hack squat mechanics. Warnings: Don't round your lower back at the bottom. Keep knees tracking over toes—avoid valgus collapse. Engage the safety catches between sets.

name: Hammer Strength Iso-Lateral Low Row
description: Setup: Sit with chest against pad, feet on footrests. Grip the low handles with palms facing each other (neutral grip). Each arm operates independently. Technique: Pull one or both handles back toward your hips, driving elbows straight back. Focus on lower lat contraction. Squeeze at peak, then return with control. Can train one arm at a time or both simultaneously. Hints: The low-pull angle specifically targets the lower lats and teres major—different from cable rows which pull to mid-torso. Iso-lateral design lets you work each side independently to fix imbalances. Keep chest on pad and avoid torso rotation when doing single-arm work. Warnings: Don't rotate torso when pulling single-arm. Avoid rounding upper back. Keep movements controlled—no jerking.

name: Hammer Strength Seated Dip
description: Setup: Sit with back against pad, feet flat on floor. Grip handles at your sides with palms facing inward. Adjust seat so arms start bent at roughly 90°. Technique: Press handles downward by extending elbows, squeezing triceps at full extension. Return with control to the starting position. Keep elbows close to body throughout. Hints: Unlike bodyweight dips, this isolates triceps without requiring shoulder stability or risking shoulder impingement. The seated position eliminates lower body momentum entirely. Focus on lockout squeeze for peak tricep contraction. Can load heavier than bodyweight dips safely. Warnings: Don't flare elbows outward. Avoid using shoulder depression to push weight—isolate the tricep extension. Don't slam the weight stack at bottom.

name: Hammer Strength Behind-the-Neck Press
description: Setup: Sit facing away from the machine with back against pad. Grip handles positioned behind head at ear level with palms facing forward. Feet flat on floor. Technique: Press handles upward and together in a converging arc until arms are fully extended overhead. Lower with control back to ear level. Keep core braced throughout. Hints: The machine's guided path makes behind-the-neck pressing safer than the free-weight version by controlling the bar path and preventing excessive shoulder external rotation. Targets lateral deltoid heads more than front presses. Excellent for building capped shoulder appearance. Start with lighter weight to assess shoulder mobility. Warnings: Skip this exercise if you have any shoulder impingement issues. Don't lower below ear level. Keep head neutral—don't push it forward.

name: Hammer Strength Pullover
description: Setup: Sit with back against pad, feet flat. Place elbows on the arm pads and grip handles above head. Seat belt optional for stability. Technique: Pull handles down in an arc from overhead to hip level using lat contraction—elbows remain fixed at approximately 90°. Squeeze lats hard at the bottom position. Return with control overhead. Hints: This is one of the few machines that isolates the lats without bicep involvement—pure lat contraction through shoulder extension. The pullover motion uniquely stretches lats at the top and contracts them at the bottom. Think 'drive elbows into your sides.' Cannot be replicated well with free weights or cables. Warnings: Don't use excessive range of motion overhead if shoulder mobility is limited. Avoid arching back off the pad. Keep the movement smooth—no jerking at the start.

name: Hammer Strength Calf Raise
description: Setup: Stand with shoulders under the pads, balls of feet on the foot platform edge, heels hanging off. Select weight on the plate-loaded lever arms. Slight knee bend for standing version, or sit for seated version. Technique: Lower heels below platform level for a full stretch. Drive up onto toes as high as possible, squeezing calves at peak for 2 seconds. Lower with a controlled 3-second eccentric. Hints: The linear lever-arm design provides consistent resistance unlike the variable resistance of standard stack machines. Plate-loaded allows for precise micro-loading. Alternate toe angles across sets (straight, inward, outward) to target different calf heads. The stretch at the bottom is key—don't short-change range of motion. Warnings: Don't bounce at the bottom—controlled stretch only. Avoid locking knees on standing version. Keep shoulders level under pads—don't lean.

name: Push-ups
description: Setup: Place your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width on the floor with your feet together and legs extended behind you. Stack your shoulders over your hands, brace your core, and squeeze your glutes so the body stays rigid from head to heels. Technique: Lower yourself under control until your chest is just above the floor between your hands. Press the floor away to return to full arm extension while keeping the elbows at roughly a 45-degree angle and the body in one line. Hints: Exhale as you press and think about moving the whole body as one unit instead of snaking up from the hips. Think about pulling the floor together with your hands to create upper-body tension before each rep. Warnings: Do not let the head drop below the chest or allow the lower back to collapse as fatigue builds. Stop the set if your hips sag, your chin juts forward, or wrist discomfort changes the pressing path.

name: Diamond Push-ups
description: Setup: Place your hands close together under your sternum with thumbs and index fingers forming a diamond on the floor with your feet set just outside hip-width if you need more balance. Stack your shoulders over your hands, brace your core, and squeeze your glutes so the body stays rigid from head to heels. Technique: Lower yourself under control until your lower chest and triceps feel loaded without your elbows flaring hard to the sides. Press the floor away to return to full arm extension while keeping the hands narrow and the elbows tracking close to your torso. Hints: If the full version is too difficult, elevate the hands slightly and keep the narrow pressing path identical. Think about pulling the floor together with your hands to create upper-body tension before each rep. Warnings: Avoid forcing the wrist angle if the diamond hand position is uncomfortable and do not collapse into the shoulders at the bottom. Stop the set if your hips sag, your chin juts forward, or wrist di...

name: Cable Crossover
description: Setup: Set both pulleys just above shoulder height and stand centered between them with one foot slightly forward. Grab the handles with a soft bend in the elbows and lift your chest without letting the ribs flare. Technique: Sweep the handles downward and inward in a wide hugging arc until the hands meet in front of the lower chest. Return slowly until you feel a loaded stretch across the pecs while keeping the elbow angle nearly unchanged. Hints: Think about bringing your biceps toward each other instead of pressing the handles with the triceps. Adjust your stance so the torso stays quiet and the cables keep tension even at the back of the movement. Warnings: Do not turn the exercise into a press by repeatedly bending and straightening the elbows. Avoid overstretching the front of the shoulders or shrugging the traps upward when the load gets heavy.

name: Dumbbell Pullover
description: Setup: Lie across a bench or lengthwise with your upper back supported and hold one dumbbell over your chest with both hands under the top plate. Plant your feet firmly, keep a slight bend in the elbows, and brace the ribs down before you start lowering. Technique: Arc the dumbbell back behind your head until you feel a stretch through the chest and lats without losing spinal position. Pull the weight back over your chest by driving the upper arms together and keeping the elbows nearly fixed. Hints: Use a smooth tempo and stop the descent where you still feel the chest working more than the shoulders or lower back. Thinking about moving from the shoulder joint rather than the hands usually makes the rep feel cleaner and more targeted. Warnings: Do not let the dumbbell drift too low if your shoulder mobility is limited or the ribs pop open excessively. Keep your neck relaxed and avoid turning the lift into a tricep extension with a large elbow bend.

name: Smith Machine Bench Press
description: Setup: Lie on a flat bench under the bar so it starts above mid-chest and take a grip slightly wider than shoulder-width. Pull your shoulder blades together, keep your ribs down, and plant your feet so the torso stays stable from the first rep to the last. Technique: Lower the load under control until the bar reaches the lower chest with your forearms still nearly vertical. Press it back up along the fixed rail over the lower chest and shoulder line rather than toward your neck while keeping the wrists stacked over the elbows and the chest tall. Hints: Use the smith path to practice even pressure through both hands and steady pauses on the chest. A brief pause near the bottom helps you own the range and prevents the movement from turning into a rebound. Warnings: Do not set the bench so far forward or backward that the fixed path forces your shoulders into a painful groove. Do not flare the elbows so wide that the shoulders lose a safe groove or let the weight drift toward your face.

name: Machine Incline Press
description: Setup: Adjust the seat so the handles line up around upper-chest height and your wrists start stacked over your elbows. Pull your shoulder blades together, keep your ribs down, and plant your feet so the torso stays stable from the first rep to the last. Technique: Lower the load under control until your elbows reach a deep but comfortable incline press position without the shoulders rolling forward. Press it back up along the machine arc until the elbows are extended without slamming into lockout while keeping the wrists stacked over the elbows and the chest tall. Hints: Keep your head and upper back glued to the pad and drive evenly through both handles instead of twisting. A brief pause near the bottom helps you own the range and prevents the movement from turning into a rebound. Warnings: Avoid cranking the seat too low, which turns the angle into a shoulder-dominant press and can irritate the front delts. Do not flare the elbows so wide that the shoulders lose a safe groove or ...

name: Svend Press
description: Setup: Stand tall and hold two light dumbbells pressed flat against each other at chest height with elbows slightly lifted. Set your feet about hip-width apart and keep constant inward pressure through the palms before the first rep begins. Technique: Press the dumbbells straight out from your sternum until the elbows are nearly straight while never letting the bells separate. Pull them back to the chest slowly and maintain the squeeze so the pecs stay loaded during the entire set. Hints: Choose lighter weights than you expect because the crushing tension is what makes the exercise effective. Think about trying to bend the dumbbells through each other and keep the shoulders down away from the ears. Warnings: Do not relax the inward pressure at lockout or the movement turns into a simple front raise or light press. If the shoulders start taking over, reduce the load and re-center the dumbbells directly in front of the chest.

name: Wide Grip Dips
description: Setup: Take a wider-than-shoulder-width grip on parallel bars and support yourself with arms straight and shoulders depressed. Lean your torso slightly forward to emphasize the chest and brace your midline so the legs stay quiet instead of swinging. Technique: Bend the elbows and lower until your upper arms reach at least parallel while you still own the shoulder position. Drive back to the top by pressing through the palms and keeping the chest proud throughout the range. Hints: Keep the elbows angled back rather than straight out to the sides even though the grip is wide. A slight pause near the bottom helps you own the stretch and keeps the rep from turning into a bounce. Warnings: A wide grip increases shoulder demand, so stop short of maximal depth if you lose control in the bottom stretch. Do not shrug at the top or drop into a depth that forces the shoulders to roll forward and lose control.

name: Feet-Elevated Push-ups
description: Setup: Place your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width on the floor with your feet supported on a bench or box behind you and your legs locked so the body stays straight despite the elevated angle. Stack your shoulders over your hands, brace your core, and squeeze your glutes so the body stays rigid from head to heels. Technique: Lower yourself under control until your upper chest approaches the floor between the hands without the lower back sagging. Press the floor away to return to full arm extension while shifting more load into the upper chest and front shoulders while maintaining a rigid torso. Hints: Set the elevation low enough that you can still keep the shoulders packed and a clean pressing line. Think about pulling the floor together with your hands to create upper-body tension before each rep. Warnings: Do not let the bench height force you into an uncontrolled dive or excessive neck extension at the bottom. Stop the set if your hips sag, your chin juts forward, or wr...

name: Single-Arm Cable Press
description: Setup: Set a single cable handle around chest height and stand in a split stance with the working side holding the handle beside your chest. Brace hard through the trunk and square the hips so the cable cannot twist you before the rep even begins. Technique: Press the handle straight forward until the elbow is extended and the hand finishes in line with the sternum. Bring it back slowly to the start while resisting rotation and keeping the shoulder blade moving smoothly on the rib cage. Hints: Think about punching through the target while the opposite side of your torso stays frozen in place. A slight reach at the end can help the pec shorten fully, but only if the shoulder does not shrug toward the ear. Warnings: Do not let the ribs flare or the torso spin open just to move more weight. If the front of the shoulder feels pinchy, reduce the range slightly and reset the cable height before continuing.

name: Incline Dumbbell Fly
description: Setup: Set an incline bench around 30 to 45 degrees and lie back with dumbbells above the upper chest, palms facing each other. Pin the upper back to the pad, keep a soft bend in the elbows, and plant the feet to stabilize the torso. Technique: Open the arms in a wide arc until you feel the upper chest stretch without the shoulders dumping forward. Bring the bells back together over the upper chest by hugging inward rather than pressing with the triceps. Hints: Keep the dumbbells traveling slightly inward as they rise so the pecs stay the prime movers throughout the set. Use lighter weights than you would for pressing and let the eccentric do the work instead of chasing numbers. Warnings: Do not lower so deep that the elbows sink behind the torso and yank on the front of the shoulder capsule. Avoid turning the rep into an incline press by repeatedly straightening and rebending the elbows.

name: Hex Press
description: Setup: Lie on a flat bench holding two dumbbells together over your chest with the inner heads touching the entire time. Retract the shoulder blades, plant the feet, and squeeze the dumbbells into each other before you start lowering. Technique: Lower the dumbbells toward the center of the chest while preserving the inward pressure and a tucked elbow path. Press back up in a straight line and continue crushing the bells together so the pecs stay active through lockout. Hints: The pressing squeeze is the main driver here, so use loads you can control without the bells drifting apart. A moderate pause one inch above the chest often increases chest tension and cleans up the groove. Warnings: Do not flare the elbows hard or let the dumbbells separate into a standard neutral-grip press. If wrist strain shows up, slightly rotate the handles until the forearms feel stacked and stable again.

name: Sumo Deadlift
description: Setup: Take a wide stance with toes turned out, place the bar over mid-foot, and grip inside your knees with a flat back. Brace hard through the trunk, pull slack out of the bar or handles, and lock the lats in before breaking the weight free. Technique: Drive through the floor and extend knees and hips together while keeping the bar close while you push the knees out and stay tall through the chest. Finish tall with glutes locked without leaning back past neutral and lower the weight under control by reversing the hinge. Hints: Think about spreading the floor apart with your feet so the hips can stay close to the bar and the torso can stay more upright. Think about pushing the ground away and keeping the load glued to your body instead of yanking with the arms. Warnings: Do not allow the knees to cave inward or the hips to shoot up so high that the lift loses its sumo mechanics. Stop the set if the lower back rounds, the hips shoot up faster than the shoulders, or the lockout turns...

name: Stiff-Leg Deadlift
description: Setup: Stand with feet hip-width apart, hold the bar against your thighs, and keep only a minimal bend in the knees. Brace hard through the trunk, pull slack out of the bar or handles, and lock the lats in before breaking the weight free. Technique: Drive through the floor and extend knees and hips together while letting the hips travel back while the bar skims your legs and the hamstrings lengthen hard. Finish tall with the spine neutral and the lats tight as you stand back up and lower the weight under control by reversing the hinge. Hints: The range is determined by hamstring flexibility and back position, not by forcing the plates to touch the floor. Think about pushing the ground away and keeping the load glued to your body instead of yanking with the arms. Warnings: Avoid turning it into a squat or bouncing the bar off the bottom range when the posterior chain is under stretch. Stop the set if the lower back rounds, the hips shoot up faster than the shoulders, or the lockout t...

name: Seated Cable Row
description: Setup: Sit tall at the cable station with knees softly bent, feet braced on the platform, and a neutral or close grip handle in your hands. Set your ribs down, keep a neutral neck, and let the shoulders move freely without losing spinal position. Technique: Pull the handle or bar toward the lower ribs or upper abdomen while the elbows travel close to the body. Pause briefly with the chest open and the shoulder blades squeezed together without leaning back excessively before lowering to a full stretch under control. Hints: Start each rep by letting the shoulder blades protract slightly so you earn the stretch instead of rowing from a shortened position. Lead the motion with the elbows and keep the torso quiet so the lats and upper back do the work instead of momentum. Warnings: Do not kick the weight with your legs or turn every rep into a full-body rock to move the stack. Avoid jerking the weight, craning the neck, or rounding the lumbar spine as fatigue builds.

name: Inverted Row
description: Setup: Set a bar in a rack around waist height and lie underneath it with hands just outside shoulder-width. Straighten your body from shoulders to heels, squeeze glutes and abs, and set the shoulder blades before pulling. Technique: Row your chest toward the bar by driving the elbows down and back while keeping the body rigid like a plank. Lower under control until the arms are straight again without letting the hips sag or the shoulders roll forward. Hints: Bending the knees makes the exercise easier, while elevating the feet increases the difficulty without changing the pulling pattern. Think about touching the lower chest to the bar and keeping your neck neutral instead of reaching with the chin. Warnings: Do not shrug your shoulders up near the ears or let the body twist as you pull. If the lower back starts arching to finish reps, shorten the set and restore full-body tension before continuing.

name: Straight-Arm Pulldown
description: Setup: Stand facing a high cable with a straight bar or rope, hinge slightly at the hips, and take the arms overhead with elbows softly unlocked. Set the chest high, keep a small lean only if needed, and start each rep by depressing the shoulder blades. Technique: Pull the handle or your arms along a long arc from overhead down toward your thighs without bending the elbows more than necessary. Finish with the hands beside the thighs and the lats fully shortened while the chest stays lifted and return slowly to a full overhead stretch without losing tension. Hints: Think about sweeping with the upper arms and locking the rib cage down so the shoulders do not compensate. Think about driving elbows toward your hips rather than curling the load with your hands. Warnings: Do not turn it into a tricep pressdown by repeatedly flexing and extending the elbows through the whole movement. Do not turn the movement into a backward row or let the shoulders shrug toward the ears at the top.

name: Meadows Row
description: Setup: Stand perpendicular to a barbell loaded in a landmine-style setup, hinge over, and grab the sleeve with a pronated grip while bracing the free hand on your thigh or a bench. Set your ribs down, keep a neutral neck, and let the shoulders move freely without losing spinal position. Technique: Pull the handle or bar toward your back hip and lower ribs while allowing a natural stretch at the bottom. Pause briefly with the elbow high and the lat fully engaged without your torso opening up before lowering to a full stretch under control. Hints: A staggered stance usually helps you stay square and keeps the row from turning into a torso rotation drill. Lead the motion with the elbows and keep the torso quiet so the lats and upper back do the work instead of momentum. Warnings: Do not yank from the floor or twist the spine aggressively just to move a heavier plate on the sleeve. Avoid jerking the weight, craning the neck, or rounding the lumbar spine as fatigue builds.

name: Pendlay Row
description: Setup: Set up over a barbell with your torso nearly parallel to the floor, shins close to vertical, and hands just outside shoulder-width. Set your ribs down, keep a neutral neck, and let the shoulders move freely without losing spinal position. Technique: Pull the handle or bar toward the lower chest or upper abdomen from a dead stop on the floor each repetition. Pause briefly with explosive but controlled intent while the torso angle stays fixed and the bar returns fully to the floor before lowering to a full stretch under control. Hints: Reset your brace and back position between reps instead of trying to bounce through touch-and-go momentum. Lead the motion with the elbows and keep the torso quiet so the lats and upper back do the work instead of momentum. Warnings: Do not stand up between reps or let the low back round just because the start position feels demanding. Avoid jerking the weight, craning the neck, or rounding the lumbar spine as fatigue builds.

name: Rack Pull
description: Setup: Set the bar on safety pins or blocks around just below or just above knee height and stand with feet hip-width under the bar. Brace hard through the trunk, pull slack out of the bar or handles, and lock the lats in before breaking the weight free. Technique: Drive through the floor and extend knees and hips together while keeping the bar tight to the thighs as you drive the hips through from the shortened range. Finish tall with the glutes squeezed and the shoulders stacked over the hips instead of cranking backward and lower the weight under control by reversing the hinge. Hints: Use the shortened range to overload the top half, but keep the same deadlift brace and patience off the pins. Think about pushing the ground away and keeping the load glued to your body instead of yanking with the arms. Warnings: Do not slam the bar into the pins between reps or start with the shoulders far behind the bar, which usually irritates the lower back. Stop the set if the lower back rounds...

name: Machine Row
description: Setup: Adjust the seat and chest pad so the handles start just in front of you and the chest stays supported throughout the set. Set your ribs down, keep a neutral neck, and let the shoulders move freely without losing spinal position. Technique: Pull the handle or bar toward the lower ribs or pockets depending on handle angle and intended back emphasis. Pause briefly with the shoulder blades drawing back and down while the chest stays glued to the pad before lowering to a full stretch under control. Hints: Use the pad to remove momentum and focus on equal drive through both arms if the machine is bilateral. Lead the motion with the elbows and keep the torso quiet so the lats and upper back do the work instead of momentum. Warnings: Do not shorten the front stretch by hovering in the contracted position or lifting your chest off the pad to finish reps. Avoid jerking the weight, craning the neck, or rounding the lumbar spine as fatigue builds.

name: Reverse Grip Lat Pulldown
description: Setup: Sit at the pulldown station with a shoulder-width supinated grip and thighs locked under the pad. Set the chest high, keep a small lean only if needed, and start each rep by depressing the shoulder blades. Technique: Pull the handle or your arms along a path toward the upper chest while the elbows stay slightly in front of the torso. Finish with the chest high and the elbows close to your sides without leaning way back and return slowly to a full overhead stretch without losing tension. Hints: The underhand grip often lets you feel more lower-lat and bicep involvement, but the rep should still start from the back. Think about driving elbows toward your hips rather than curling the load with your hands. Warnings: Do not pull behind the neck or curl the bar down with the wrists and forearms leading the motion. Do not turn the movement into a backward row or let the shoulders shrug toward the ears at the top.

name: Smith Machine Row
description: Setup: Stand over the smith bar in a hinged position with knees soft and hands around shoulder-width on the fixed bar path. Set your ribs down, keep a neutral neck, and let the shoulders move freely without losing spinal position. Technique: Pull the handle or bar toward the lower chest or upper abdomen while keeping the torso angle steady against the bar's fixed track. Pause briefly with a squeezed upper back and the elbows driving behind the body before a controlled descent before lowering to a full stretch under control. Hints: Experiment with foot placement so the smith path lines up with your natural row groove instead of forcing your shoulders forward. Lead the motion with the elbows and keep the torso quiet so the lats and upper back do the work instead of momentum. Warnings: Do not set your stance so close that the bar crashes into your knees or so far away that you must round to reach it. Avoid jerking the weight, craning the neck, or rounding the lumbar spine as fatigue bu...

name: Chest-Supported Dumbbell Row
description: Setup: Lie face down on an incline bench with dumbbells hanging under your shoulders and your chest firmly planted on the pad. Set your ribs down, keep a neutral neck, and let the shoulders move freely without losing spinal position. Technique: Pull the handle or bar toward your lower ribs or hips depending on whether you want more upper-back or lat bias. Pause briefly with the chest heavy on the pad and the shoulder blades moving through a full squeeze at the top before lowering to a full stretch under control. Hints: Let the arms hang completely at the bottom so each rep starts from a true stretch rather than a shortened shrug. Lead the motion with the elbows and keep the torso quiet so the lats and upper back do the work instead of momentum. Warnings: Do not lift your chest off the bench or kick the feet into the floor to create momentum once the set gets hard. Avoid jerking the weight, craning the neck, or rounding the lumbar spine as fatigue builds.

name: Single-Arm Cable Row
description: Setup: Set a single handle around lower-rib height and take a split stance or seated position that lets your torso stay square to the cable. Set your ribs down, keep a neutral neck, and let the shoulders move freely without losing spinal position. Technique: Pull the handle or bar toward the side of your lower ribs while the elbow tracks close to the torso. Pause briefly with the shoulder blade wrapping around the rib cage on the reach and then retracting hard at peak contraction before lowering to a full stretch under control. Hints: Reach slightly at the front without collapsing posture so you get a better lat stretch before every pull. Lead the motion with the elbows and keep the torso quiet so the lats and upper back do the work instead of momentum. Warnings: Do not rotate open or twist through the low back just to make the handle travel farther behind you. Avoid jerking the weight, craning the neck, or rounding the lumbar spine as fatigue builds.

name: Wide Grip Lat Pulldown
description: Setup: Take a wide pronated grip on the bar, sit tall, and lock your thighs under the pad before you start the set. Set the chest high, keep a small lean only if needed, and start each rep by depressing the shoulder blades. Technique: Pull the handle or your arms along a path toward the upper chest with elbows driving down and out in the same plane as the bar. Finish with the chest proud and the shoulder blades pulled down rather than shrugged up toward the ears and return slowly to a full overhead stretch without losing tension. Hints: Use a grip wide enough to bias the upper back and lats, but not so wide that your elbows cannot travel through a strong range. Think about driving elbows toward your hips rather than curling the load with your hands. Warnings: Do not yank the bar behind your neck or turn the last half of the rep into a lower-back swing to touch your chest. Do not turn the movement into a backward row or let the shoulders shrug toward the ears at the top.

name: Renegade Row
description: Setup: Set two dumbbells on the floor and assume a high plank with feet wider than hip-width for balance and the hands gripping the handles. Brace the abs and glutes hard so the hips and ribs stay square to the floor before you lift either dumbbell. Technique: Row one dumbbell toward the hip by driving the elbow up while the opposite arm presses strongly into the floor. Lower it under control, reset your plank, and then repeat on the other side without shifting your torso. Hints: Widening the feet usually improves stability and lets you challenge the row instead of fighting to stay upright. Imagine balancing a glass of water on your lower back so every rep stays honest and anti-rotational. Warnings: Do not twist the hips open or shrug the shoulder of the rowing arm toward the ear to finish the rep. If the plank position breaks down first, reduce the load or elevate the hands so you can keep a clean body line.

name: Trap Bar Deadlift
description: Setup: Step into the center of the trap bar with feet about hip-width apart and grip the neutral handles after hinging down with a flat back. Brace hard through the trunk, pull slack out of the bar or handles, and lock the lats in before breaking the weight free. Technique: Drive through the floor and extend knees and hips together while keeping the chest up and the handles traveling straight with your midline as you stand. Finish tall with the glutes engaged and the shoulders stacked directly over the hips and lower the weight under control by reversing the hinge. Hints: Use the neutral handles to stay tight through the upper back and drive evenly through both legs rather than yanking with the arms. Think about pushing the ground away and keeping the load glued to your body instead of yanking with the arms. Warnings: Do not let the knees cave or start with the hips so low that the lift turns into a loose squat with the bar floating forward. Stop the set if the lower back rounds, th...

name: Band Pull-Apart
description: Setup: Hold a light to moderate resistance band straight out in front of your chest with palms down and arms nearly straight. Stand tall with ribs down and shoulders set so the band starts level with your sternum rather than high up by the neck. Technique: Pull the band apart by moving your arms out to the sides until the band touches or nears your chest. Return slowly to the starting width without losing tension or letting the shoulders roll forward. Hints: Think about spreading the floor through your feet and pulling with the rear delts and mid-back instead of just the hands. Bands get easier at the front, so keep intent on the return and do not let the last few inches go slack. Warnings: Do not shrug into the upper traps or crank the arms far behind the torso in search of fake range. If you feel it mostly in the neck, lower the band height a little and reduce the tension before continuing.

name: Front Squat
description: Setup: Rack the bar across the front delts with elbows high, hands outside the shoulders, and feet around shoulder-width apart. Fill the trunk with air, brace hard, and spread the floor with your feet so knees and hips stay organized throughout the rep. Technique: Descend under control until your hips drop between the heels while the elbows stay lifted and the torso remains upright. Drive through the full foot to stand while keeping the elbows up and the chest proud as you push the floor away. Hints: A clean-grip rack or cross-arm rack both work if you can keep the bar stable on the shoulders instead of in the hands. Use the eccentric to find balance first and then accelerate upward once you own the bottom position. Warnings: Do not let the elbows collapse down or the bar roll forward off the delts when the set gets challenging. Do not let the knees cave inward or force depth that makes the pelvis tuck and the lower back lose neutrality.

name: Goblet Squat
description: Setup: Hold a kettlebell by the horns at chest level with elbows pointed down and feet set at a comfortable squat stance. Fill the trunk with air, brace hard, and spread the floor with your feet so knees and hips stay organized throughout the rep. Technique: Descend under control until your elbows travel inside the knees and you reach the deepest position you can control with a neutral spine. Drive through the full foot to stand while keeping the kettlebell close to the chest and the full foot rooted into the floor. Hints: Use the bell as a counterbalance to sit down between the hips and practice clean torso positioning. Use the eccentric to find balance first and then accelerate upward once you own the bottom position. Warnings: Do not let the kettlebell drift away from your body or collapse the chest just to chase extra depth. Do not let the knees cave inward or force depth that makes the pelvis tuck and the lower back lose neutrality.

name: Hack Squat Machine
description: Setup: Set your shoulders under the pads, place your back firmly against the support, and position your feet about shoulder-width on the platform. Fill the trunk with air, brace hard, and spread the floor with your feet so knees and hips stay organized throughout the rep. Technique: Descend under control until your thighs reach at least parallel or deeper if your hips and back stay planted against the pad. Drive through the full foot to stand while driving evenly through the platform while the knees track over the toes. Hints: A slightly lower foot placement usually biases the quads more, while a higher placement can feel friendlier on the knees for some lifters. Use the eccentric to find balance first and then accelerate upward once you own the bottom position. Warnings: Do not let the hips peel off the pad or slam into the machine stops at the bottom of the rep. Do not let the knees cave inward or force depth that makes the pelvis tuck and the lower back lose neutrality.

name: Bulgarian Split Squat
description: Setup: Stand a stride in front of a bench with your rear foot elevated behind you and dumbbells held at your sides. Square the hips, brace your trunk, and keep most of the tension on the working leg before each rep. Technique: Lower until the rear knee drops toward the floor and the front thigh reaches a deep but controlled angle. Drive through the working foot to stand back up through the front heel without bouncing off the rear leg while keeping balance and alignment. Hints: Keep most of your weight over the front leg and let the rear foot act as a kickstand instead of a second driver. A controlled descent and a firm tripod foot usually improve stability more than simply taking lighter or heavier weights. Warnings: Do not set up so narrow that you feel unstable or so long that the front hip gets yanked into an uncomfortable stretch. Do not crash into the bottom or let the front knee collapse inward as you change direction.

name: Step-ups
description: Setup: Stand in front of a box or bench that puts your working thigh around parallel when the whole foot is planted on top and hold dumbbells at your sides. Square the hips, brace your trunk, and keep most of the tension on the working leg before each rep. Technique: Lower until the working leg reaches full foot pressure on the box and the trailing foot only lightly follows. Drive through the working foot to stand tall on the box by driving through the planted foot and controlling the way back down while keeping balance and alignment. Hints: Lean slightly forward from the hips to stay over the working leg rather than launching upward from the trailing foot. A controlled descent and a firm tripod foot usually improve stability more than simply taking lighter or heavier weights. Warnings: Do not push hard off the floor with the back leg or use a box height that makes you twist the hips to get on top. Do not crash into the bottom or let the front knee collapse inward as you change dire...

name: Reverse Lunges
description: Setup: Stand tall with dumbbells at your sides and feet hip-width apart before stepping one leg back. Square the hips, brace your trunk, and keep most of the tension on the working leg before each rep. Technique: Lower until both knees bend to roughly ninety degrees while the front foot stays fully planted. Drive through the working foot to return to standing by pushing through the front heel and bringing the rear leg back under you while keeping balance and alignment. Hints: A reverse step usually feels more knee-friendly than a forward lunge, especially if you keep the front shin controlled. A controlled descent and a firm tripod foot usually improve stability more than simply taking lighter or heavier weights.
