Warnings: Do not overstride so far that you lose balance or let the front knee collapse inward as you stand. Do not crash into the bottom or let the front knee collapse inward as you change direction.

name: Hip Thrust
description: Setup: Sit on the floor with your upper back against a bench, roll a padded barbell into the hip crease, and plant your feet about shoulder-width apart. Tuck the chin slightly, brace the abs, and plant the feet firmly so the movement starts from the hips instead of the lower back. Technique: Drive through the heels to drive the hips upward until the torso and thighs form a straight line. Pause at the top with the glutes squeezed hard and the ribs still down instead of the lower back arching before lowering under control. Hints: Tuck the chin slightly and look forward to keep a cleaner spine position through the top half of the rep. Shins that are nearly vertical at the top usually make it easier to load the glutes without cramping the hamstrings. Warnings: Do not jam the feet too far forward or backward or chase lockout by hyperextending the lumbar spine. Do not overextend the lumbar spine to fake lockout or let the knees collapse inward as the hips rise.

name: Glute Bridge
description: Setup: Lie on the floor with a padded barbell in your hip crease, knees bent, and feet planted around hip-width apart. Tuck the chin slightly, brace the abs, and plant the feet firmly so the movement starts from the hips instead of the lower back. Technique: Drive through the heels to bridge the hips off the floor until the knees, hips, and shoulders line up. Pause at the top with your pelvis slightly tucked and the glutes fully shortened before you lower back down before lowering under control. Hints: Because the range is shorter than a hip thrust, use a deliberate pause at the top to make every rep count. Shins that are nearly vertical at the top usually make it easier to load the glutes without cramping the hamstrings. Warnings: Do not push through the toes or let the knees flare wildly outward just to force more height. Do not overextend the lumbar spine to fake lockout or let the knees collapse inward as the hips rise.

name: Good Morning
description: Setup: Set a barbell on your upper back like a squat, stand with feet around hip-width, and unlock the knees slightly. Unlock the knees slightly, brace the midsection, and keep the chest long so the movement comes from the hips rather than the spine. Technique: Hinge back until your torso is near parallel to the floor or until the hamstrings reach their end range while the back stays flat. Drive the hips forward to return while squeezing the glutes and bringing the torso back to vertical without rushing. Hints: Start light and think about bowing forward from the hips rather than folding through the spine. Keep the load close to your center of mass and think about closing a car door with your hips on every rep. Warnings: Do not load this like a squat until you can maintain a rigid brace and consistent bar position across the entire range. Never chase extra range by rounding the back or snapping the hips through so hard that the ribs flare at the top.

name: Sissy Squat
description: Setup: Stand upright while holding a support lightly with one hand if needed and rise onto the balls of your feet. Keep the hips extended, squeeze the glutes, and prepare to let the knees travel forward as the torso leans back in one line. Technique: Lower by bending the knees and letting them travel forward while the body stays long from shoulders to knees. Drive back up through the balls of the feet and quads until you return to the tall start position. Hints: Think about opening the front of the thighs and keeping the hips from folding, because this is a knee-dominant squat pattern. Use a small range at first and progress slowly until the knees feel comfortable with the deep forward travel. Warnings: Do not drop quickly into the bottom or attempt full depth if you cannot control the knees through the entire motion. If you feel sharp pain around the kneecap or the support leg starts wobbling badly, stop and regress the movement.

name: Smith Machine Squat
description: Setup: Set the bar across your upper traps, position your feet slightly in front of the bar path if needed, and stand tall under the smith machine. Fill the trunk with air, brace hard, and spread the floor with your feet so knees and hips stay organized throughout the rep. Technique: Descend under control until your thighs reach at least parallel while the fixed bar path still lets your hips and knees track smoothly. Drive through the full foot to stand while driving through the full foot and matching the machine path without letting the hips shoot backward. Hints: Take a set or two to find the foot position that best matches the rail path instead of forcing your usual free-bar stance. Use the eccentric to find balance first and then accelerate upward once you own the bottom position. Warnings: Do not let the fixed track pull you onto your toes or jam your knees because your feet are set too far forward or back. Do not let the knees cave inward or force depth that makes the pelvis t...

name: Box Squat
description: Setup: Set up with a barbell on your back and a box behind you that matches the depth you want to train. Fill the trunk with air, brace hard, and spread the floor with your feet so knees and hips stay organized throughout the rep. Technique: Descend under control until you sit back to the box with control, lightly touch or sit without collapsing, and keep tension through the trunk. Drive through the full foot to stand while driving up aggressively from the box while keeping the chest up and the knees tracking out. Hints: Use the box as a depth target and tension tool, not as a place to fully relax and rock for momentum. Use the eccentric to find balance first and then accelerate upward once you own the bottom position. Warnings: Do not crash onto the box or lose your brace before the concentric, because that removes the point of the exercise and stresses the spine. Do not let the knees cave inward or force depth that makes the pelvis tuck and the lower back lose neutrality.

name: Donkey Calf Raise
description: Setup: Position yourself in the donkey calf raise machine with hips hinged, shoulders or lower back under the pad, and the balls of your feet on the platform edge. Set your hips and shoulders so you can move only through the ankles instead of bouncing with the knees or torso. Technique: Lower until the calves reach a full loaded stretch with the heels dropping below the platform. Drive onto the balls of your feet and pause with a hard calf contraction at the very top while you stay balanced over the feet before descending again. Hints: A slight bend at the knees can help keep the focus on the calves instead of locking out through the joints. A slow eccentric and a hard top squeeze usually build more calf tension than adding sloppy partial reps. Warnings: Do not bounce through the bottom stretch or let the lower back round while you chase more range. Do not bounce out of the bottom or roll the ankles inward or outward while the load is on your feet.

name: Single Leg Press
description: Setup: Sit in the leg press with your back and head against the pad and place one foot in the center of the platform while the other rests safely off to the side. Grip the handles, brace the trunk, and make sure the loaded knee lines up with the middle toes before releasing the safety. Technique: Lower the platform under control until the working leg reaches a deep but pain-free bend and the pelvis stays anchored to the seat. Press the sled back up through the full foot of the working leg without twisting the hips or locking the knee violently. Hints: Keep the unused leg quiet and focus on driving evenly through the heel and mid-foot of the working side. Because the movement is unilateral, slightly lighter loads usually let you hold better hip alignment and knee tracking. Warnings: Do not let the lower back peel off the pad or the platform rotate because the foot is placed off-center. If the knee caves inward during the ascent, stop the set and reduce the load until you can control ...

name: Nordic Hamstring Curl
description: Setup: Kneel on a pad with your ankles secured under a stable support or by a partner and keep the body tall from knees to shoulders. Cross the arms over the chest or hold them ready in front of you so you can catch yourself lightly if needed. Technique: Lower your body forward as one rigid unit by resisting the fall with the hamstrings for as long as possible. Catch with your hands if necessary, give a small push from the floor, and use the hamstrings to pull back to the start. Hints: Think about dragging your heels into the anchor the whole time to keep the posterior chain switched on. Partial ranges and assisted reps are completely valid until you build the strength to control more of the descent. Warnings: Do not break at the hips or snap down uncontrolled once you pass the sticking point. If you feel cramping or knee discomfort, reduce the range, use more assistance, and make sure the anchoring setup is secure.

name: Kettlebell Swing
description: Setup: Stand a foot behind the kettlebell, hinge down to grip it, and hike it back between your legs with a long neutral spine. Create whole-body tension before every rep so the bell moves from crisp hip power instead of loose arms and a floppy torso. Technique: Explosively extend the hips with a sharp hip snap that floats the bell to chest height instead of lifting it with the shoulders. Receive or finish the bell with the glutes locked and the arms relaxed like straps as the bell reaches its apex and drops back into the next hinge and recycle into the next rep under control. Hints: Think of it as a violent plank at the top followed by a patient hinge on the way down. A fast, vertical zip of the bell close to the body usually feels safer and stronger than casting it far away from you. Warnings: Do not squat the swing, hyperextend the back at the top, or let the bell drift far below the knees on the backswing. Do not hyperextend at the top or yank with the lower back and shoulders w...

name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
description: Setup: Stand with dumbbells in front of your thighs, feet hip-width apart, and a soft bend in the knees. Unlock the knees slightly, brace the midsection, and keep the chest long so the movement comes from the hips rather than the spine. Technique: Hinge back until the dumbbells reach mid-shin or the point where the hamstrings are fully loaded and your back is still flat. Drive the hips forward to return while keeping the dumbbells close to your legs and the shoulders packed down. Hints: Because each arm works independently, use the dumbbells to find a symmetrical hinge and even pressure through both feet. Keep the load close to your center of mass and think about closing a car door with your hips on every rep. Warnings: Do not let the dumbbells drift far away from the shins or chase range by rounding through the thoracic and lumbar spine. Never chase extra range by rounding the back or snapping the hips through so hard that the ribs flare at the top.

name: Cossack Squat
description: Setup: Stand in a very wide stance with toes slightly turned out and shift your weight into one leg while the other leg stays extended. Lift your chest, brace your core, and keep the entire foot of the working leg rooted before initiating the descent. Technique: Sit down over one hip until the working knee bends deeply and the opposite leg stays long with the heel contacting the floor if mobility allows. Drive through the bent leg to return to center and repeat to the same side or alternate sides with control. Hints: Use your arms as a counterbalance in front of you and move slowly enough to feel the adductors and glutes loading on the working side. If ankle or groin mobility limits depth, elevate the heel of the working side slightly and build range over time. Warnings: Do not force the extended leg into a position that twists the knee or causes groin pain. Avoid collapsing inward on the working knee or dropping the torso so low that you lose the tall squat posture.

name: Curtsy Lunge
description: Setup: Stand tall with dumbbells at your sides and step one leg diagonally behind the other into a curtsy stance. Square the hips, brace your trunk, and keep most of the tension on the working leg before each rep. Technique: Lower until the front thigh and glute feel loaded while the rear knee drops behind and slightly outside the front heel line. Drive through the working foot to push through the front foot and bring the rear leg back to the start without wobbling while keeping balance and alignment. Hints: Keep the hips mostly square to the front and let the front glute control the descent and ascent rather than twisting through the spine. A controlled descent and a firm tripod foot usually improve stability more than simply taking lighter or heavier weights. Warnings: Do not overcross so far that the front knee caves sharply inward or balance becomes the limiting factor of the set. Do not crash into the bottom or let the front knee collapse inward as you change direction.

name: Barbell Walking Lunge
description: Setup: Set a barbell across your upper back, stand tall, and create a tight upper-back shelf before stepping into the first lunge. Square the hips, brace your trunk, and keep most of the tension on the working leg before each rep. Technique: Lower until both knees bend under control and the torso stays stacked over the hips as you move forward. Drive through the working foot to push through the front foot and bring the rear leg through into the next step with a stable trunk while keeping balance and alignment. Hints: Short, deliberate steps usually keep the bar quieter on your back and make the set feel more repeatable. A controlled descent and a firm tripod foot usually improve stability more than simply taking lighter or heavier weights. Warnings: Do not rush the walk or let the front knee slam forward while the bar tips you into a folded torso. Do not crash into the bottom or let the front knee collapse inward as you change direction.

name: Machine Standing Calf Raise
description: Setup: Stand under the shoulder pads of the machine with the balls of your feet on the platform edge and the heels hanging free. Set your hips and shoulders so you can move only through the ankles instead of bouncing with the knees or torso. Technique: Lower until you feel a strong stretch through the calves without losing balance or pressure on the forefoot. Drive onto the balls of your feet and pause with the calves fully shortened while the knees stay softly unlocked and the torso steady before descending again. Hints: Pause at both ends of the range so the machine cannot dictate the tempo for you. A slow eccentric and a hard top squeeze usually build more calf tension than adding sloppy partial reps. Warnings: Do not bounce off the bottom or lock the knees rigidly to create artificial range through the ankle joint. Do not bounce out of the bottom or roll the ankles inward or outward while the load is on your feet.

name: Arnold Press
description: Setup: Sit or stand with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms facing you, and elbows slightly in front of the torso. Brace your abs and glutes so the rib cage stays stacked over the pelvis instead of flaring open. Technique: Move the weight with a smooth rotation from palms-in to palms-forward as you press the dumbbells overhead. Finish with the biceps beside the ears and the wrists stacked over the elbows when viewed from the side and lower back to the start under control. Hints: Keep the forearms moving around the dumbbells instead of flaring the elbows wildly during the rotation. Keep wrists stacked over elbows and move your head only enough to clear a vertical path rather than pressing around your face. Warnings: Do not rush the turn at the bottom or overarch your back to create room for the weights to travel. Do not overarch the lower back or turn the rep into a layback press when the weight gets heavy.

name: Machine Shoulder Press
description: Setup: Adjust the seat so the handles start around ear to shoulder height and your forearms are vertical at the bottom. Brace your abs and glutes so the rib cage stays stacked over the pelvis instead of flaring open. Technique: Move the weight with a steady press through the machine path while both shoulders stay pinned to the pad. Finish with the elbows extended without slamming into the machine's end range or losing posture and lower back to the start under control. Hints: Use the seat and back support to focus on clean upward drive and equal effort through both handles. Keep wrists stacked over elbows and move your head only enough to clear a vertical path rather than pressing around your face. Warnings: Do not crane the neck forward or let one side dominate the press just because the machine provides extra stability. Do not overarch the lower back or turn the rep into a layback press when the weight gets heavy.

name: Push Press
description: Setup: Rack a barbell on the upper chest with hands just outside shoulder-width and feet around hip-width apart. Brace your abs and glutes so the rib cage stays stacked over the pelvis instead of flaring open. Technique: Move the weight with a shallow vertical dip followed by aggressive leg drive that launches the bar into the overhead press. Finish with the bar fixed over mid-foot with the glutes and abs tight as you stand tall and lower back to the start under control. Hints: Keep the dip short and straight down so the legs add speed without turning the movement into a squat. Keep wrists stacked over elbows and move your head only enough to clear a vertical path rather than pressing around your face. Warnings: Do not dip forward, press with soft knees, or catch the bar overhead with the ribs flared and heels shifting. Do not overarch the lower back or turn the rep into a layback press when the weight gets heavy.

name: Upright Row
description: Setup: Stand tall holding a barbell in front of your thighs with a grip just inside shoulder-width and elbows pointed down. Brace the abs, soften the knees, and keep the shoulders relaxed before initiating the pull. Technique: Lead with the elbows and pull the bar upward close to the body until the upper arms reach about shoulder height. Lower under control along the same path and keep tension in the delts and upper traps instead of dropping the bar back to the thighs. Hints: Think elbows high and wrists quiet so the shoulders drive the movement rather than the forearms yanking the bar. A slightly wider grip often feels friendlier on the shoulders if a narrow grip pinches near the top. Warnings: Do not pull so high that the shoulders jam into an uncomfortable internally rotated position. If you feel sharp anterior shoulder discomfort, shorten the range or switch to a different variation.

name: Smith Machine Overhead Press
description: Setup: Set a bench upright or stand under the smith bar with hands just outside shoulder-width and the bar starting around chin or upper-chest height. Brace your abs and glutes so the rib cage stays stacked over the pelvis instead of flaring open. Technique: Move the weight with the fixed bar path while you move your head enough to let the bar travel vertically. Finish with the arms straight overhead and the shoulders packed rather than shrugged into the ears and lower back to the start under control. Hints: Take time to line up your feet or bench position with the smith track so the groove feels natural and repeatable. Keep wrists stacked over elbows and move your head only enough to clear a vertical path rather than pressing around your face. Warnings: Do not sit or stand so far away from the rail path that you must press around your face or crank the wrists backward. Do not overarch the lower back or turn the rep into a layback press when the weight gets heavy.

name: Bradford Press
description: Setup: Take a barbell from the rack with a normal overhead-press grip and hold it at the upper chest while standing tall. Brace your abs and glutes so the rib cage stays stacked over the pelvis instead of flaring open. Technique: Move the weight with a partial press over the head followed by a controlled pass behind the head and then back to the front without locking out. Finish with continuous shoulder tension as the bar travels front to back in a smooth arc and lower back to the start under control. Hints: Use a much lighter load than a normal press and keep the movement deliberate because the time under tension is the point. Keep wrists stacked over elbows and move your head only enough to clear a vertical path rather than pressing around your face. Warnings: Do not slam the bar behind your neck or attempt this variation if your shoulder mobility cannot support the transition cleanly. Do not overarch the lower back or turn the rep into a layback press when the weight gets heavy.

name: Lu Raise
description: Setup: Stand with light dumbbells at your sides and palms facing inward or slightly back with the torso tall and still. Set a soft bend in the elbows, keep the ribs down, and let the shoulder blades move naturally instead of pinning them rigidly. Technique: Lift the load along a wide arc out to the sides and upward until the dumbbells finish overhead or as high as your mobility allows. Lower slowly while keeping the elbows softly bent and the traps from taking over the movement. Hints: This is a precision raise, so use very light weights and own the entire path instead of muscling through it. Use the longest clean range you can own and think about reaching away from your body rather than just yanking up. Warnings: Do not force the last few degrees of elevation if your shoulders lose smooth upward rotation or the rib cage flares open. Do not swing the torso or crank the shoulders upward to force range that your joints cannot control.

name: Plate Front Raise
description: Setup: Stand holding a single dumbbell by one head in front of your thighs with both hands or hold a pair of light dumbbells if that setup feels better. Set a soft bend in the elbows, keep the ribs down, and let the shoulder blades move naturally instead of pinning them rigidly. Technique: Lift the load along a path straight in front of the body until the hands reach shoulder height or slightly higher with control. Lower slowly while keeping the torso quiet and the shoulders away from the ears as the load returns to the start. Hints: Exhale as the weight rises and think about reaching forward rather than swinging upward from the lower back. Use the longest clean range you can own and think about reaching away from your body rather than just yanking up. Warnings: Do not lean back to cheat the first half of the rep or whip the weight down once it reaches the top. Do not swing the torso or crank the shoulders upward to force range that your joints cannot control.

name: Kettlebell Clean
description: Setup: Stand over the kettlebell with feet about hip-width and hike it back between your legs after taking a solid hinge position. Create whole-body tension before every rep so the bell moves from crisp hip power instead of loose arms and a floppy torso. Technique: Explosively extend the hips with a crisp hip extension that sends the bell upward close to your torso rather than looping away from you. Receive or finish the bell with the bell softly rotating around the hand into the rack position on the forearm with the elbow close to the body and recycle into the next rep under control. Hints: Keep the elbow tucked and think zipper up the body so the bell lands quietly instead of slamming onto the wrist. A fast, vertical zip of the bell close to the body usually feels safer and stronger than casting it far away from you. Warnings: Do not curl the bell with the arm or overextend the lower back trying to force the rack position. Do not hyperextend at the top or yank with the lower back ...

name: Kettlebell Snatch
description: Setup: Set up exactly like a swing or clean with a strong hinge, a firm grip, and the bell hiked back high between the legs. Create whole-body tension before every rep so the bell moves from crisp hip power instead of loose arms and a floppy torso. Technique: Explosively extend the hips with an explosive hip snap that sends the bell up close to the body before it turns over overhead. Receive or finish the bell with the arm locked out overhead with the shoulder packed and the wrist neutral before guiding the bell back down and recycle into the next rep under control. Hints: Punch through at the top so the bell wraps around the hand instead of flipping over and crashing on the forearm. A fast, vertical zip of the bell close to the body usually feels safer and stronger than casting it far away from you. Warnings: Do not cast the bell forward in a big arc or finish with a loose overhead position and flared ribs. Do not hyperextend at the top or yank with the lower back and shoulders whe...

name: Trap Bar Shrug
description: Setup: Stand inside a trap bar holding the handles at your sides with feet about hip-width apart and chest tall. Lock the elbows straight, brace the abs, and let the shoulders sit naturally at the bottom before each rep. Technique: Elevate the shoulders straight up toward the ears without bending the elbows or rocking through the torso. Pause at the top for a strong trap squeeze and then lower slowly to a full stretch at the bottom. Hints: Think straight up and straight down rather than rolling the shoulders, because the traps respond best to vertical motion. The neutral handles often let you use solid load, but the top squeeze should still control the rep instead of momentum. Warnings: Do not circle the shoulders or bounce the trap bar off your thighs to initiate the shrug. If your neck feels jammed, reduce the load and keep the head stacked over the torso rather than jutting forward.

name: Banded Face Pull
description: Setup: Anchor a resistance band at upper-face height and grip it with thumbs pointing behind you as you step back to create tension. Stand tall with ribs down and start with arms extended so the band pulls straight toward your face. Technique: Pull the band toward the bridge of your nose while driving the elbows high and rotating the hands apart. Return slowly to full arm extension and let the shoulder blades protract slightly before beginning the next rep. Hints: Think elbows wide and thumbs behind you so the rear delts and upper back do the work instead of the biceps. Bands get lighter near the finish, so pause at peak contraction to make the shortened position count. Warnings: Do not arch the lower back or jut the chin forward to meet the band. If you feel mostly neck tension, lower the shoulders, use less band tension, and keep the line of pull centered on eye level.

name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
description: Setup: Sit or stand with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms forward, and elbows stacked under the wrists. Brace your abs and glutes so the rib cage stays stacked over the pelvis instead of flaring open. Technique: Move the weight with a smooth bilateral press straight overhead without letting the dumbbells drift way out to the sides. Finish with the arms locked out overhead and the shoulders stable rather than shrugged and lower back to the start under control. Hints: Drive both bells up at the same speed and keep the forearms vertical at the bottom for a stronger path. Keep wrists stacked over elbows and move your head only enough to clear a vertical path rather than pressing around your face. Warnings: Do not overarch the lower back or smash the dumbbells together overhead if that throws your shoulders out of position. Do not overarch the lower back or turn the rep into a layback press when the weight gets heavy.

name: Cable Front Raise
description: Setup: Stand facing away from a low cable with a handle in one or both hands and start with the arm slightly behind the body. Set a soft bend in the elbows, keep the ribs down, and let the shoulder blades move naturally instead of pinning them rigidly. Technique: Lift the load along a path straight in front of the torso until the hand reaches shoulder height with constant cable tension. Lower slowly while controlling the descent all the way back into the loaded stretch behind the body. Hints: The cable provides tension at the bottom, so stay patient there and do not yank through the first few inches. Use the longest clean range you can own and think about reaching away from your body rather than just yanking up. Warnings: Do not lean away from the stack or let the shoulder shrug upward to finish the top portion of the rep. Do not swing the torso or crank the shoulders upward to force range that your joints cannot control.

name: Cuban Press
description: Setup: Stand with light dumbbells in front of your thighs and pull them up into a high-elbow upright-row position around lower-chest height. Keep the ribs stacked and the shoulders centered because this movement demands more control than load. Technique: Externally rotate the dumbbells upward until the forearms point vertical and then press them overhead in one smooth sequence. Reverse the pattern under control by lowering to the rotated position and then back down to the start. Hints: Use extremely light weights and prioritize smooth external rotation because the goal is shoulder quality and deltoid work, not max load. Moving slowly between the row, rotation, and press phases usually keeps the shoulder path cleaner and safer. Warnings: Do not yank the dumbbells up with momentum or force the rotation if your shoulders pinch during the turnover. If the movement loses sequence or the elbows drop suddenly, reset and lighten the load before continuing.

name: Dumbbell Y Raise
description: Setup: Lie face down on an incline bench or hinge forward with light dumbbells hanging under the shoulders and thumbs pointing up. Set a soft bend in the elbows, keep the ribs down, and let the shoulder blades move naturally instead of pinning them rigidly. Technique: Lift the load along a Y-shaped path up and out in front of the body until the upper arms align with your torso or slightly above. Lower slowly while keeping the neck long and the shoulder blades moving upward naturally instead of shrugging hard. Hints: The thumbs-up position usually feels better on the shoulders and helps bias the lower traps and upper delts. Use the longest clean range you can own and think about reaching away from your body rather than just yanking up. Warnings: Do not use heavy weights or swing from the lumbar spine because this variation is built for precision, not load. Do not swing the torso or crank the shoulders upward to force range that your joints cannot control.

name: EZ-Bar Curl
description: Setup: Stand holding an EZ-bar with a comfortable underhand grip on the angled portions and elbows close to your sides. Set the shoulder blades down and keep the upper arm position honest so elbow flexion drives the movement. Technique: Curl the load along an arc toward the front of your shoulders while the upper arms stay mostly vertical. Pause at the top with the biceps fully shortened without the shoulders rolling forward before lowering to full elbow extension under control. Hints: Choose the grip angle that keeps your wrists happy and lets you move through the whole range under control. Squeezing the handle hard and controlling the eccentric usually makes lighter weights feel more productive than swinging heavier ones. Warnings: Do not lean back into a cheat curl or let the elbows drift so far forward that tension leaves the biceps. Do not rock your torso, let the elbows drift far forward, or shorten the bottom range to avoid the hard part.

name: Concentration Curl
description: Setup: Sit on a bench with your feet wide and brace the back of your upper arm against the inside of your thigh while holding a dumbbell. Set the shoulder blades down and keep the upper arm position honest so elbow flexion drives the movement. Technique: Curl the load along a tight path toward your shoulder while the upper arm stays pinned in place. Pause at the top with a hard bicep squeeze and your wrist stacked neutrally over the forearm before lowering to full elbow extension under control. Hints: Let the dumbbell stretch fully at the bottom before each rep because the fixed arm position already limits your ability to cheat. Squeezing the handle hard and controlling the eccentric usually makes lighter weights feel more productive than swinging heavier ones. Warnings: Do not slam the elbow into the thigh or hunch your torso so much that the shoulder rolls forward and shortens the range. Do not rock your torso, let the elbows drift far forward, or shorten the bottom range to avoid...

name: Spider Curl
description: Setup: Lie face down on an incline bench with your chest supported and arms hanging straight down while holding an EZ-bar. Set the shoulder blades down and keep the upper arm position honest so elbow flexion drives the movement. Technique: Curl the load along a path straight up toward your forehead while the upper arms stay angled slightly forward. Pause at the top with peak contraction with the elbows still fixed and the chest heavy on the pad before lowering to full elbow extension under control. Hints: Because the bench removes momentum, focus on squeezing and lowering slowly rather than chasing extra weight. Squeezing the handle hard and controlling the eccentric usually makes lighter weights feel more productive than swinging heavier ones. Warnings: Do not lift your chest off the bench or let the shoulders take over just to finish the last inches of the rep. Do not rock your torso, let the elbows drift far forward, or shorten the bottom range to avoid the hard part.

name: Bayesian Cable Curl
description: Setup: Attach a single handle to a low cable, face away from the stack, and step forward so your working arm starts slightly behind your torso. Set the shoulder blades down and keep the upper arm position honest so elbow flexion drives the movement. Technique: Curl the load along a long path from behind the body toward the front of your shoulder while keeping the elbow near a fixed point. Pause at the top with the biceps fully shortened without losing the stretch created by the cable at the bottom before lowering to full elbow extension under control. Hints: The behind-the-body start is the magic of this variation, so keep tension there and resist the urge to step back toward the stack. Squeezing the handle hard and controlling the eccentric usually makes lighter weights feel more productive than swinging heavier ones. Warnings: Do not twist the torso or let the shoulder roll forward just to make the handle travel farther. Do not rock your torso, let the elbows drift far forward, or...

name: Machine Bicep Curl
description: Setup: Adjust the seat so your elbows line up with the machine pivot and your upper arms rest comfortably on the pad. Set the shoulder blades down and keep the upper arm position honest so elbow flexion drives the movement. Technique: Curl the load along the machine arm through its full path without lifting the elbows off the support. Pause at the top with a strong contraction at the top and the forearms lowering under full control to the bottom before lowering to full elbow extension under control. Hints: Use the machine stability to focus on full range and symmetrical effort between sides rather than bouncing through the stack. Squeezing the handle hard and controlling the eccentric usually makes lighter weights feel more productive than swinging heavier ones. Warnings: Do not jam the shoulders forward or set the seat too low or high, because that changes the joint line and makes the curl feel awkward. Do not rock your torso, let the elbows drift far forward, or shorten the bottom...

name: Cable Overhead Tricep Extension
description: Setup: Face away from a high cable with a rope or single handle, bring the hands overhead, and keep the elbows pointed forward. Brace your trunk and set the shoulders so the upper arms stay stable instead of drifting around as fatigue builds. Technique: Extend the elbows along an overhead arc until the arms are straight above or slightly in front of the head. Finish with the triceps fully shortened while the upper arms stay mostly fixed beside the ears and return under control until you feel the triceps lengthen again. Hints: A split stance often makes it easier to keep the ribs down and the low back quiet during overhead extensions. Keep tension through the entire set by moving only at the elbow unless the exercise is intentionally designed to include some shoulder motion. Warnings: Do not let the elbows flare wide or the lower back arch just to finish the last part of lockout. Do not flare the ribs, jam the elbows into an uncomfortable angle, or snap through lockout with uncontrol...

name: Dumbbell Kickback
description: Setup: Hinge forward with a flat back, hold a dumbbell in one hand, and bring the upper arm in line with your torso. Brace your trunk and set the shoulders so the upper arms stay stable instead of drifting around as fatigue builds. Technique: Extend the elbows along a short path behind you until the elbow is completely straight. Finish with the tricep locked hard while the upper arm stays fixed beside your ribs and return under control until you feel the triceps lengthen again. Hints: Light to moderate weight works best because the tension comes from precision in the short top range rather than load. Keep tension through the entire set by moving only at the elbow unless the exercise is intentionally designed to include some shoulder motion. Warnings: Do not let the elbow drop during the set or swing the dumbbell from the shoulder to create fake extension. Do not flare the ribs, jam the elbows into an uncomfortable angle, or snap through lockout with uncontrolled speed.

name: Overhead Dumbbell Tricep Extension
description: Setup: Sit or stand holding one dumbbell by the top plate overhead with both hands and elbows pointing mostly forward. Brace your trunk and set the shoulders so the upper arms stay stable instead of drifting around as fatigue builds. Technique: Extend the elbows along a long overhead path until the elbows are straight and the dumbbell sits above the crown of the head. Finish with the triceps fully shortened while the ribs stay stacked over the pelvis and return under control until you feel the triceps lengthen again. Hints: Keep the biceps close to the ears and lower only as far as you can maintain elbow alignment and shoulder comfort. Keep tension through the entire set by moving only at the elbow unless the exercise is intentionally designed to include some shoulder motion. Warnings: Do not flare the elbows dramatically or let the dumbbell drift behind you so far that the shoulders lose control. Do not flare the ribs, jam the elbows into an uncomfortable angle, or snap through loc...

name: JM Press
description: Setup: Lie on a flat bench with a barbell over your chest using a medium-close grip and shoulder blades pinned to the bench. Start with arms straight and then allow the elbows to travel slightly forward as you prepare to lower the bar. Technique: Lower the bar diagonally toward the upper chest or throat line by bending mainly at the elbows while keeping the upper arms relatively high. Press the bar back up by extending the elbows and guiding it back to the starting line over the shoulders. Hints: The movement sits between a close-grip press and a skull crusher, so use moderate loads and learn the elbow path before pushing hard. A smooth diagonal descent usually keeps tension on the triceps far better than dropping the bar straight down like a bench press. Warnings: Do not let the elbows flare wildly or drop the bar too low on the neck or face line during the descent. If the wrists fold back or the shoulders feel unstable, reduce the load and tighten your upper-back setup before the ...

name: Farmer's Walk
description: Setup: Pick up a heavy dumbbell in each hand from the floor or blocks and stand fully upright before moving. Stand tall with the chin tucked, shoulders packed, and abs braced before you take the first step. Technique: Walk with short deliberate steps with the shoulders packed and the dumbbells hanging quietly at your sides. Maintain a tall posture with the ribs down, abs tight, and grip crushing the handles until the distance is complete until the set ends and then set the weights down with control. Hints: Think about getting taller with each step and let your breathing stay controlled instead of panicked and noisy. Shorter, deliberate steps usually let you carry more load with better posture than rushing the distance. Warnings: Do not let the dumbbells bang against your thighs or speed-walk so fast that posture and foot placement fall apart. Do not lean dramatically from side to side or let grip failure make you drop the implements carelessly.

name: Reverse Wrist Curl
description: Setup: Sit on a bench and rest your forearms on your thighs or a pad with palms facing down while holding a barbell. Support the forearms so the wrists can move cleanly without the shoulders or elbows taking over the rep. Technique: Move through the wrists with lifting the backs of the hands toward your forearms through wrist extension. Squeeze briefly at the top with the forearm extensors fully engaged without the elbows lifting off the support before lowering to a full stretch under control. Hints: Use small, strict reps and keep the grip secure but not death-gripped so the wrists can move smoothly. Smaller ranges performed slowly usually light up the forearms better than trying to fling the bar or dumbbells. Warnings: Do not turn the movement into an elbow lift or drop the wrists quickly into the bottom stretch under a heavy bar. Do not crank into painful wrist extension or let the load roll into your fingers in a way that strains the joints.

name: Zottman Curl
description: Setup: Stand with dumbbells at your sides and palms facing forward with elbows close to the torso. Set the shoulder blades down and keep the upper arm position honest so elbow flexion drives the movement. Technique: Curl the load along a standard supinated curl on the way up and then rotating to palms-down for the slow eccentric. Pause at the top with the biceps squeezed at the top before you pronate the forearms for the lowering phase before lowering to full elbow extension under control. Hints: The rotation is the point of the exercise, so slow down the turnover instead of rushing from up to down. Squeezing the handle hard and controlling the eccentric usually makes lighter weights feel more productive than swinging heavier ones. Warnings: Do not fling the dumbbells up or skip the pronated lowering phase, because that removes the forearm emphasis that makes the variation unique. Do not rock your torso, let the elbows drift far forward, or shorten the bottom range to avoid the hard...

name: EZ-Bar Skull Crusher
description: Setup: Lie on a flat bench holding an EZ-bar over your shoulders with a comfortable angled grip and elbows pointing upward. Brace your trunk and set the shoulders so the upper arms stay stable instead of drifting around as fatigue builds. Technique: Extend the elbows along a controlled arc toward your forehead or just behind the head depending on shoulder comfort. Finish with the elbows fully extended and the triceps squeezed without the upper arms drifting excessively and return under control until you feel the triceps lengthen again. Hints: Lowering slightly behind the head often gives a better tricep stretch than stopping over the forehead, as long as the elbows stay happy. Keep tension through the entire set by moving only at the elbow unless the exercise is intentionally designed to include some shoulder motion. Warnings: Do not let the elbows flare wildly or bounce out of the bottom range when the bar approaches your head. Do not flare the ribs, jam the elbows into an uncomfor...

name: EZ-Bar Preacher Curl
description: Setup: Sit at a preacher bench with your upper arms fully supported on the pad and hands on the EZ-bar angled grips. Set the shoulder blades down and keep the upper arm position honest so elbow flexion drives the movement. Technique: Curl the load along the bar up the pad toward your shoulders while keeping the armpits anchored to the bench. Pause at the top with the biceps shortened hard without lifting the elbows off the support before lowering to full elbow extension under control. Hints: Control the last third of the lowering phase because preacher work gets challenging exactly where the biceps are long. Squeezing the handle hard and controlling the eccentric usually makes lighter weights feel more productive than swinging heavier ones. Warnings: Do not hyperextend the elbows at the bottom or use body English by lifting your chest off the pad. Do not rock your torso, let the elbows drift far forward, or shorten the bottom range to avoid the hard part.

name: EZ-Bar Reverse Curl
description: Setup: Stand with an overhand grip on the EZ-bar and wrists aligned comfortably with the angled handle sections. Set the shoulder blades down and keep the upper arm position honest so elbow flexion drives the movement. Technique: Curl the load along a path toward the upper chest while the knuckles stay facing forward. Pause at the top with the forearms and brachialis fully contracted while the elbows remain close to the torso before lowering to full elbow extension under control. Hints: Keep the wrists flat and think about lifting with the top of the forearm instead of rolling the bar into the fingers. Squeezing the handle hard and controlling the eccentric usually makes lighter weights feel more productive than swinging heavier ones. Warnings: Do not let the shoulders rock backward or the wrists collapse into extension as the bar gets near the top. Do not rock your torso, let the elbows drift far forward, or shorten the bottom range to avoid the hard part.

name: Cable Curl
description: Setup: Stand facing a low cable with a straight bar or handles in your hands and elbows close to your sides. Set the shoulder blades down and keep the upper arm position honest so elbow flexion drives the movement. Technique: Curl the load along a smooth path toward the shoulders while maintaining tension from the cable at both ends of the rep. Pause at the top with the biceps fully shortened and the elbows still pinned near the torso before lowering to full elbow extension under control. Hints: Step back far enough that the stack never fully unloads and your arms start with tension rather than slack. Squeezing the handle hard and controlling the eccentric usually makes lighter weights feel more productive than swinging heavier ones. Warnings: Do not let the cable pull your shoulders forward at the bottom or swing the torso to blast through the sticking point. Do not rock your torso, let the elbows drift far forward, or shorten the bottom range to avoid the hard part.

name: Reverse-Grip Pushdown
description: Setup: Stand facing a high cable with an underhand grip on a straight or short bar and elbows tucked near your ribs. Brace your trunk and set the shoulders so the upper arms stay stable instead of drifting around as fatigue builds. Technique: Extend the elbows along a straight downward path until the elbows lock out by your sides. Finish with the triceps fully shortened and the wrists neutral rather than overly bent back and return under control until you feel the triceps lengthen again. Hints: The underhand grip usually limits load, so focus on elbow control and a hard squeeze instead of chasing the stack. Keep tension through the entire set by moving only at the elbow unless the exercise is intentionally designed to include some shoulder motion. Warnings: Do not let the elbows drift forward or the shoulders roll inward just to move heavier weight on the cable. Do not flare the ribs, jam the elbows into an uncomfortable angle, or snap through lockout with uncontrolled speed.

name: Cross-Body Hammer Curl
description: Setup: Stand with dumbbells at your sides using a neutral grip and shoulders set down and back. Set the shoulder blades down and keep the upper arm position honest so elbow flexion drives the movement. Technique: Curl the load along each dumbbell diagonally across the body toward the opposite side of the chest while the thumb stays up. Pause at the top with the brachialis and brachioradialis lit up at the top without the elbow flying far forward before lowering to full elbow extension under control. Hints: The cross-body path often feels best when you keep the elbow close to the torso and stop just short of shoulder involvement. Squeezing the handle hard and controlling the eccentric usually makes lighter weights feel more productive than swinging heavier ones. Warnings: Do not swing the torso or turn the movement into a front raise by lifting the elbow high in front of you. Do not rock your torso, let the elbows drift far forward, or shorten the bottom range to avoid the hard part.

name: Tate Press
description: Setup: Lie on a flat bench with a dumbbell in each hand and start with arms extended over the chest, palms facing your feet. Bring the dumbbells close together above the chest and keep the elbows wide but still under control before lowering. Technique: Lower the heads of the dumbbells toward the chest by bending at the elbows while the upper arms stay relatively fixed. Extend the elbows to return the dumbbells to the start without letting them drift far apart or turn into a press. Hints: Think about tipping the dumbbells in toward the chest and then driving through the triceps to reverse the motion. Light to moderate weights usually feel best because this variation loads the triceps through a short but demanding elbow path. Warnings: Do not let the shoulders roll forward or the elbows flare so aggressively that the front delts take over. If the wrists feel awkward, adjust the dumbbell angle slightly but keep the movement centered around elbow extension.

name: Dumbbell Wrist Curl
description: Setup: Sit on a bench and rest your forearms on your thighs or a bench with palms up while holding light dumbbells or one dumbbell per hand. Support the forearms so the wrists can move cleanly without the shoulders or elbows taking over the rep. Technique: Move through the wrists with curling the palms upward through wrist flexion while the forearms stay supported. Squeeze briefly at the top with the flexors fully shortened and the fingers wrapped around the handle without death-gripping before lowering to a full stretch under control. Hints: Keep the movement small and smooth so the wrist flexors do the work rather than the elbows rocking up and down. Smaller ranges performed slowly usually light up the forearms better than trying to fling the bar or dumbbells. Warnings: Do not drop quickly into the bottom stretch or use weights so heavy that the dumbbells roll in the hands and strain the wrists. Do not crank into painful wrist extension or let the load roll into your fingers in a ...

name: Bicycle Crunch
description: Setup: Lie on your back with hands lightly by your temples, shoulder blades just off the floor, and hips and knees bent to tabletop. Flatten the ribs toward the pelvis and breathe out enough that the abs can actually shorten rather than just the neck flexing. Technique: Curl the torso with bringing one shoulder toward the opposite knee while the other leg extends long. Return with switching sides with control and keeping the abs braced instead of collapsing between reps so you keep tension without simply collapsing to the floor. Hints: Move slowly enough that the rib cage rotates and the abs stay engaged rather than just pedaling the legs wildly. Think about bringing the sternum toward the pelvis instead of yanking on your head or flinging the legs around. Warnings: Do not yank on the head with your hands or arch the lower back every time the extending leg reaches long. Do not pull on the neck, arch the lower back to create fake range, or rush the set so breathing and control disapp...

name: Hanging Leg Raise
description: Setup: Hang from a pull-up bar with arms straight, shoulder blades slightly depressed, and legs together underneath you. Set the pelvis in a slight posterior tilt so the abs start engaged before the legs ever move. Technique: Lift the legs with lifting the straight or slightly bent legs until they reach hip height or higher while the pelvis curls under. Lower with a slow eccentric and a quiet torso instead of swinging back and forth under the bar until just before the lower back wants to arch away from your brace. Hints: Start each rep by tucking the pelvis, not by kicking the legs, if you want the abs to lead the movement. If straight legs are too demanding, bend the knees slightly and own the pelvic control before progressing. Warnings: Do not swing, kip, or lose your shoulder position by hanging passively as the legs rise and fall. Do not swing, kip, or let the hip flexors completely take over while the abdominal brace disappears.

name: Lying Leg Raise
description: Setup: Lie flat on your back with hands by your sides or lightly under your hips and legs straight together. Set the pelvis in a slight posterior tilt so the abs start engaged before the legs ever move. Technique: Lift the legs with lifting the legs by curling the pelvis slightly and keeping the lower back pressed gently into the floor. Lower with control until the heels hover just above the floor or the point where your back would start to arch until just before the lower back wants to arch away from your brace. Hints: Exhale hard as the legs rise and think about drawing the zipper of your pants toward your ribs. If straight legs are too demanding, bend the knees slightly and own the pelvic control before progressing. Warnings: Do not drop the legs quickly or let the low back peel off the floor just to gain extra range at the bottom. Do not swing, kip, or let the hip flexors completely take over while the abdominal brace disappears.

name: Cable Woodchopper
description: Setup: Stand sideways to a cable stack with the handle set high or low depending on the angle you want and feet about shoulder-width apart. Brace the trunk and keep the hips square so the movement comes from the intended segment instead of everything twisting at once. Technique: Perform the rep with rotating through the torso and shoulders as you pull the handle across the body in a chopping arc. Finish each repetition with the abs braced and the hips controlled instead of spinning wildly on every rep before returning to the start under control. Hints: Let the rib cage and upper trunk rotate, but keep the knees soft and the pelvis organized so you own the path. Exhaling through the hard part often helps lock the ribs down and improves the quality of the brace. Warnings: Do not yank with the arms or twist through the lumbar spine while the feet stay glued in an awkward position. Do not chase range by jerking through the spine or letting the shoulders shrug and the pelvis rotate wildly.

name: Ab Wheel Rollout
description: Setup: Kneel on a pad with both hands on the ab wheel directly under your shoulders and your hips slightly tucked under. Brace the abs, squeeze the glutes, and create a long line from knees to shoulders before rolling out. Technique: Roll the wheel forward as your shoulders travel in front of the hands and your body lengthens under tension. Pull back to the start by driving the hands into the floor and using the abs to keep the ribs from flaring open. Hints: Only roll as far as you can maintain a hollow body shape because extra distance without control stops targeting the abs. Exhaling hard on the way back often helps you regain trunk stiffness and finish the rep cleanly. Warnings: Do not let the lower back sag or the hips drop beneath the shoulders as the wheel moves away from you. If the full rollout is too hard, shorten the range rather than turning the rep into a shoulder stretch with no abdominal tension.

name: Mountain Climbers
description: Setup: Start in a strong high plank with hands under shoulders, legs straight behind you, and the body braced in one line. Set the shoulders and ribs before you begin so the movement comes from the hips and trunk rather than a collapsed plank. Technique: Drive one knee toward your chest while the other leg stays extended and the hands push firmly into the floor. Switch legs smoothly or quickly depending on the goal, but keep the hips level and the trunk rigid throughout. Hints: Move at a pace you can control because clean mountain climbers train the core far better than frantic half-reps. Think about pulling the knee in with your abs instead of hopping the feet and letting the shoulders drift behind the hands. Warnings: Do not let the lower back sag or pike up as fatigue builds and breathing gets harder. If the wrists or shoulders get irritated, elevate the hands slightly and keep the same body line.

name: Reverse Crunch
description: Setup: Lie on your back with knees bent around ninety degrees and legs raised so the hips and knees are stacked. Flatten the ribs toward the pelvis and breathe out enough that the abs can actually shorten rather than just the neck flexing. Technique: Curl the torso with curling the pelvis off the floor and bringing the knees toward the chest rather than swinging them toward the face. Return with control until the tailbone returns lightly to the floor and the abs stay engaged so you keep tension without simply collapsing to the floor. Hints: Think tailbone up, not knees forward, if you want the lower abs to stay involved through the shortened range. Think about bringing the sternum toward the pelvis instead of yanking on your head or flinging the legs around. Warnings: Do not build momentum by kicking the legs or letting the low back arch hard at the bottom between reps. Do not pull on the neck, arch the lower back to create fake range, or rush the set so breathing and control disapp...

name: Dead Bug
description: Setup: Lie on your back with arms straight over the shoulders and hips and knees bent to ninety degrees in tabletop. Flatten the ribs and gently press the lower back toward the floor before moving any limb. Technique: Slowly extend one leg and the opposite arm away from the body while keeping the trunk completely still. Return to the start and alternate sides without letting the pelvis tip or the rib cage flare upward. Hints: Move as if you are balancing a bowl of water on your stomach and trying not to spill it. Shorten the reach if needed because a smaller range with a solid brace beats a long range with a popping low back. Warnings: Do not rush from side to side or let the head and neck strain forward off the floor while you focus on the limbs. If the lower back lifts, reset the breath and reduce the reach length until control returns.

name: Pallof Press
description: Setup: Stand sideways to a cable stack with the handle held at your sternum and feet about shoulder-width apart or in a split stance. Brace the trunk and keep the hips square so the movement comes from the intended segment instead of everything twisting at once. Technique: Perform the rep with pressing the handle straight out from your chest while resisting the cable's pull to rotate you. Finish each repetition with the arms extended and the torso completely square before bringing the handle back in before returning to the start under control. Hints: Imagine zipping your ribs to your pelvis and spreading the floor with your feet every time the handle moves away from you. Exhaling through the hard part often helps lock the ribs down and improves the quality of the brace. Warnings: Do not twist toward the stack or lean away from it to create the illusion of stability. Do not chase range by jerking through the spine or letting the shoulders shrug and the pelvis rotate wildly.

name: Side Plank
description: Setup: Set up on one forearm or straight hand with your legs stacked and your body forming a straight line from head to heels. Position the supporting shoulder directly over the elbow or wrist and brace the glutes before lifting the hips. Technique: Raise the hips off the floor and hold the body rigid while the bottom oblique and shoulder stabilize you. Maintain long alignment through the neck and spine until the hold ends, then lower under control rather than collapsing. Hints: Think about driving the floor away and lifting the underside waist toward the ceiling to keep the hold active. If the stacked-leg version is too hard, bend the knees and build time there before progressing. Warnings: Do not let the top shoulder roll forward or the hips sag behind the line of the torso during the hold. Stop the set if shoulder discomfort replaces the intended side-core effort and adjust the setup before trying again.

name: Turkish Get-Up
description: Setup: Lie on your back holding a kettlebell locked out over one shoulder with the same-side knee bent and the opposite arm and leg angled on the floor. Pack the shoulder, keep your eyes on the bell, and organize each body segment before you start moving to the next phase. Technique: Roll to the elbow, post to the hand, bridge the hips, sweep the leg through, and rise to standing one controlled checkpoint at a time. Reverse every step carefully on the way down so the kettlebell remains stacked over the shoulder throughout the entire repetition. Hints: Treat the get-up like a moving plank and own every pause before progressing to the next position. Practice with very light load first because the sequence, not the weight, determines whether the rep is productive and safe. Warnings: Do not rush the transitions or let the kettlebell drift away from a vertical stack over the shoulder. name: Hollow Body Hold
description: Setup: Lie on your back and press the lower back into the floor before lifting the shoulders, arms, and legs slightly off the ground. Reach the arms overhead or by your sides depending on strength, and keep the ribs knitted down the entire time. Technique: Hold the hollow position by maintaining one curved line from fingertips to toes with the abs fully braced. Breathe shallowly through the hold while keeping the lower back pinned to the floor rather than arching between breaths. Hints: Shorten the lever by bending the knees or lowering the arms if you cannot maintain a true hollow shape. Think about pulling the front of the rib cage toward the pelvis and making the body feel like a banana facing the floor. Warnings: Do not let the low back pop up or the chin jut forward aggressively as the hold becomes difficult. Once position is lost, end the set instead of turning the exercise into a loose flutter-kick variation.

name: V-Up
description: Setup: Lie flat with arms extended overhead and legs straight together while the lower back rests lightly against the floor. Brace the abs before moving so the torso and legs lift together instead of one end whipping the other upward. Technique: Lift the torso and legs at the same time until your hands reach toward your shins or toes in a clean V shape. Lower under control until the shoulders and heels nearly touch down while the abs stay active and the lower back remains organized. Hints: Exhale hard as you fold and think about meeting in the middle with both ends of the body rather than flinging either one first. If straight legs turn the exercise into a momentum drill, bend the knees slightly and keep the same controlled shape. Warnings: Do not yank the neck forward or use momentum from the arms and legs if you cannot keep the movement smooth. Avoid letting the lower back arch hard at the bottom or collapsing flat between repetitions just to steal rest.

name: Plank Shoulder Tap
description: Setup: Set up in a high plank with feet slightly wider than normal and hands directly under the shoulders. Brace the abs and glutes so the body forms a straight line before lifting a hand from the floor. Technique: Tap one shoulder with the opposite hand while keeping the hips and ribs as still as possible. Return the hand to the floor softly, reestablish full pressure through both arms, and alternate sides. Hints: Wider feet make the anti-rotation demand easier, so adjust the stance before sacrificing trunk control. Press the support hand hard into the floor and imagine your belt buckle staying pointed at the ground. Warnings: Do not rock side to side or let the low back sag each time a hand leaves the floor. If the shoulders ache more than the trunk works, elevate the hands or shorten the set so quality stays high.

name: Bear Crawl
description: Setup: Start on hands and knees with toes tucked, knees hovering an inch or two off the floor, and the spine neutral. Push the floor away through the shoulders and brace the abs so the torso stays quiet before you begin moving. Technique: Crawl forward by moving opposite hand and foot together in short controlled steps while the knees remain low to the floor. Keep the hips level and the trunk rigid as you continue for distance or time without letting the knees touch down. Hints: Small steps are usually better than big reaches because they preserve position and keep tension on the entire trunk. Breathe behind the brace and think stealthy and quiet rather than fast and sloppy. Warnings: Do not let the hips pike high, the lower back sag, or the shoulders shrug up near the ears while crawling. If the knees repeatedly hit the floor or balance disappears, shorten the distance and rebuild the posture first.
